titleStmt.title: Quantum hydrodynamics of coupled electron-nuclear systems

affiliation.orgName: Istituto di Scienze e Tecnologie Molecolari
Institute of Physical and Theoretical Chemistry

analytic.title: Quantum hydrodynamics of coupled electron-nuclear systems

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: The quantum dynamics of electron-nuclear systems is analyzed from the perspective of the exact factorization of the wavefunction, with the aim of defining gauge invariant equations of motion for both the nuclei and the electrons. For pure states this is accomplished with a quantum hydrodynamical description of the nuclear dynamics and electronic density operators tied to the fluid elements. For statistical mixtures of states the exact factorization approach is extended to two limiting situations that we call "type-n" and "type-e" mixtures, depending on whether the nuclei or the electrons are, respectively, in an intrinsically mixed state. In both cases a fully gauge invariant formulation of the dynamics is obtained again in hydrodynamic form with the help of mechanical momentum moments (MMMs). Nuclear MMMs extend in a gauge invariant way the ordinary momentum moments of the Wigner distribution associated with a density matrix of positional variables, electron MMMs are operator-valued and represent a generalization of the (conditional) density operators used for pure states. The theory presented here bridges exact quantum dynamics with several mixed quantum-classical approaches currently in use to tackle non-adiabatic molecular problems, offering a foundation for systematic improvements. It further connects to non-adiabatic theories in condensed-phase systems. As an example, we re-derive the finite-temperature theory of electronic friction of Dou, Miao & Subotnik (Phys. Rev. Lett. 119, 046001 (2017)) from the dynamics of "type-e" mixtures and discuss possible improvements.

div.head: I
div.p: . Introduction 1 II. QHD with gauge fields 3 A. Momentum and velocity fields 4 B. QHD for a particle in 3D space with gauge fields 5 C. Many-particle QHD with gauge fields 7 D. Statistical mixtures 8 III. Quantum Hydrodynamics of exactly factorized electrons-nuclei 10 A. Pure states 10 B. Type-n statistical mixtures 13 C. Type-e statistical mixtures 14 D. Remark on mechanical momentum moments 17 IV. Applications 17 A. Mixed quantum-classical dynamics 17 B. Electronic friction at finite temperature 21 V. Summary and Conclusions 24 A. Circulation theorem 25 B. Wigner function and momentum moments 26 C. Gauge invariant formulation of the pure-state electron dynamics 30 * rocco.martinazzo

div.head: I. INTRODUCTION
div.p: Describing the correlated behavior of electrons and nuclei beyond the Born-Oppenheimer (BO) or adiabatic approximations poses a formidable challenge with significant implications in various fields, ranging from the photochemistry and photophysics of molecular systems, aggregates, and functional materials [1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8] to chemical kinetics at metallic surfaces [9][10][11][12][13][14] and the transport of charge through molecular junctions, mesoscopic devices, and bulk materials [15][16][17][18][19][20][21][22][23]. The BO (adiabatic) approximation significantly simplifies calculations by assuming that only one electronic state is populated. This simplification enables the complete separation of the electronic and nuclear problems. However, when the underlying assumptions no longer hold the brute-force approach of incorporating the relevant electronic states in a Born-Huang-like expansion of the total wavefunction [24] is impractical for all but small-sized systems. The limitation mainly arises because the density of electronic states increases with system size, and the electronic spectrum eventually coalesces into a continuum in an extended system. Furthermore, solving the nuclear quantum dynamical problem becomes soon impractical when increasing the system size, even for single-surface (i.e., BO) problems. Therefore, only numerical methods based on quantum-classical or quantum-semiclassical approximations, where electrons are treated quantum mechanically while nuclei are treated classically or quasi-classically, can offer promise for handling large systems. This is reasonable for many applications since the atomic nuclei considered are often heavy enough to behave as classical objects, exhibiting only weak nuclear quantum effects. The latter can thus be addressed separately, for example through a fully quantum approach within either a reduced dimensionality description or a surrogate model.

This classical treatment of the nuclear dynamics is suitable for single-surface dynamics, especially when investigating 'global' observables for which self-averaging occurs and quantum features are washed out. However, it remains questionable for non-adiabatic dynamics, even for nuclei that would behave as classical particles in a singlesurface dynamics under similar conditions. The challenge lies in determining the appropriate forces acting on the nuclei and understanding the correct nuclear influence on the quantum dynamics of the electrons. These challenges become increasingly more intricate when a larger number of electronic states are involved in the coupled dynamics. To make progress, it is essential to depart from the BO framework and consider the electronic problem in the time domain rather than the energy domain. However, defining the appropriate equations of motion is not straightforward, and different approaches emerge depending on when the classical limit for the nuclei is introduced [5].

In this context, the exact factorization (EF) of the total electron-nuclear wavefunction, as introduced by Abedi et al. [25,26], represents a groundbreaking approach. It offers an exact scheme with a built-in separation of variables that can be harnessed to account for their distinct dynamics. Nevertheless, one must handle the EF equations of motion with care when devising approximations, as the nuclear and electronic wavefunctions are not unique, and it is crucial to ensure that any derived scheme is independent of this arbitrariness. The objective of this paper is precisely to remove this arbitrariness and to reformulate the EF dynamical equations in a manner that eliminates this issue entirely.

The proposed approach is ideally suited for introducing a classical description for the nuclei while maintaining a quantum treatment of the electronic motion. It can be viewed as an extension of De Broglie-Bohm-Madelung hydrodynamics to multi-state dynamics, which is exact for any number of electronic states. Additionally, this approach allows extensions of the EF method to investigate dynamical processes involving statistical mixtures of states, thereby enabling the study of more realistic scenarios like those appearing at finite temperatures.

The EF is an "intermediate" representation that consists in projecting the total wavefunction of a molecu-lar system |Ψ in the position basis of nuclear variables, henceforth denoted as {|x }. This defines a local (i.e., position dependent) electronic state |u(x) and a nuclear wavefunction ψ(x), upon requiring that |u(x) remains normalized at any time, namely
div.formula: x|Ψ X = ψ(x) |u(x)
div.p: where the subscript X reminds that integration is performed over nuclear variables only. In other words, in EF the total wavefunction is exactly expanded as
div.formula: |Ψ t = ˆdxψ t (x) |x |u t (x)(1)
div.p: This form closely resembles the adiabatic ansatz -where the |u t 's of Eq. 1 are replaced by eigenstates of the local electronic Hamiltonians -and it is thus an ideal starting point for addressing a non-adiabatic dynamics. The equations of motion for ψ(x) and |u(x) can be obtained from the total Hamiltonian in coordinate representation for the nuclear variables which, in rather general terms, can be taken of the form
div.formula: Ĥ = T + H el (x)(2)
div.p: where T is the nuclear kinetic energy operator
div.formula: T = 1 2 ij ξ ij pi pj with pj = -i ∂ j
div.p: ξ ij is an inverse mass tensor for the nuclei and H el (x) is the electronic operator with the nuclei clamped at a configuration x. In this way, one obtains an effective Schrödinger equation for the nuclear wavefunction
div.formula: H eff ψ = i ∂ψ ∂t(3)
div.p: and an equation of motion for the electronic wavefunction
div.formula: i (∂ t + iA 0 ) |u = (H el -E) |u + K[ψ] |u(4)
div.p: In the above equations the effective Hamiltonian H eff for the nuclear dynamics closely resembles the adiabatic Hamiltonian and is specified below (Eq. 6), E = u|H el |u is the average electronic energy and A 0 = i u|∂ t u is an arbitrary (but real) term that guarantees norm conservation to the electronic wavefunction.

The detailed derivation of the equations of motion along these lines can be found in Ref. [27,28]. Here, we focus on the expression of K[ψ], the functional of ψ that subsumes the electron-nuclear coupling and that reads as
div.formula: K[ψ] |u = -i j V j Q |∂ j u -R |u(5)
div.p: where Q = 1 -|u u|, V j = (v j ψ)/ψ is a complex-valued nuclear velocity field (to be discussed below, in Sec. II A), R |u = 2 ij ξ ij D ij |u , and
div.formula: D ij |u = iA i Q |∂ j u + iA j Q |∂ i u + Q |∂ i ∂ j u
div.p: One key feature of the exact factorization approach is, as mentioned above, some freedom in defining the electron and the nuclear wavefunctions appearing in Eq. 1, namely in the choice of their phase. This gauge freedom introduces spurious terms in the corresponding equations of motion and hampers the physical interpretation of the dynamics, for which only gauge invariant quantities can be relevant. The equations above, Eqs. 3 and 4, are "microscopically" gauge covariant, meaning that they have separate, gauge covariant contributions, as discussed at length in Refs. [27,28]. We shall show below how to recast them in a form that is fully gauge invariant and that can thus display the true, physical couplings between nuclei and electrons.

The paper is organized as follows. In Section II we review the configuration-space hydrodynamical formulation of quantum mechanics developed by De Broglie, Bohm and Madelung [29,30]-henceforth, quantum hydrodynamics (QHD) -with particular emphasis on gauge fields and on the issue of gauge invariance. We address both pure and mixed states and derive a gauge invariant formulation of the dynamics for either cases. This Section is instrumental to Section III where we consider the specific problem of the nuclear -electron dynamics, in the exact factorization framework. There, the main focus is on the electron dynamics since the description of the nuclear dynamics follows from Section II. Again, we address both the pure-and the mixed-state cases and extend the exact factorization to two limiting situations where either the nuclei or the electrons are in intrinsically mixed states. Next, in Section IV we present applications of the theory to electron-nuclear dynamics beyond the Born-Oppenheimer approximation. The purpose of this Section is to show that the present theory forms a unifying framework for non-adiabatic problems, encompassing molecular and condensed-phase problems, from few electronic states to dense manifolds. The emphasis is on the mixed quantum-classical limit, where electrons are treated quantally and nuclei are described classically. Couplings between the two variables are here introduced from the exact equations of motion, with no ad hoc assumptions, and commonly adopted approaches are framed in one and the same picture. Finally, in Section V we summarize the main findings and draw some conclusions.

div.head: II. QHD WITH GAUGE FIELDS
div.p: As mentioned above, in the exact-factorization approach a key ingredient is represented by the (gauge invariant) velocity fields of the nuclei appearing in the functional of Eq. 5. They subsume the effect that the nuclear dynamics has on the evolution of the (local) electronic states, beyond the ordinary potential coupling term built in the electronic Hamiltonian H el (see Eq. 2). Here, these fields are described in their natural setting, which is that of the hydrodynamical view of quantum mechanics. We refer in particular to the configuration space hydrodynamical picture set by the (closely related) De Broglie -Bohm and Madelung views [31]. Specifically, we shall focus on the latter view, as it offers a complete gaugeinvariant formulation of quantum mechanics, which is a central concern for our aims.

In this Section we shall consider a generic pseudoelectromagnetic Hamiltonian in Schrödinger representation for a number D of degrees of freedom x = (x 1 , x 2 , ..x D ) t subjected to gauge fields. At a later stage it will play the role of effective Hamiltonian for the marginal probability amplitude describing the nuclei in the exact factorization approach (Eq. 3). This Hamiltonian takes the form
div.formula: H = ij ξ ij 2 πi πj + V -A 0(6)
div.p: where ξ ij is a (coordinate-independent) inverse mass tensor, πj = -i ∂ j -A j and A j , V are local operators depending on x and eventually on time t. Here, V is taken to be gauge invariant, while the gauge potentials A k and A 0 are required to transform according to
div.formula: A k → A k + ∂ k ϕ k = 0, 1, ..D
div.p: under the U (1) gauge transformation ψ → ψ ′ = e iϕ ψ appropriate for a spinless wavefunction (here k = 0 denotes the time coordinate, ∂ 0 ≡ ∂ t ). In the exact factorization approach V = u|H el |u + φ FS , where the first term is the instantaneous Born-Oppenheimer potential and φ FS is its "diagonal correction"
div.formula: φ FS = 2 2 ij ξ ij g ij
div.p: where g ij is the Fubini-Study metric tensor [32], the real part of the (instantaneous) quantum geometric tensor [33]
div.formula: q ij = ∂ i u|Q|∂ j u
div.p: The latter is a key object since it subsumes the gauge fields introduced by the electronic wavefunction, in a gauge invariant way. The Hamiltonian of Eq. 6 is gauge invariant in form, meaning that the transformed Hamiltonian H → H ′ ≡ -∂ t ϕ + e iϕ He -iϕ is the generator of the dynamics in the new gauge, as can be easily verified with a direct calculation i ∂ t e iϕ ψ = -(∂ t ϕ)ψ ′ + e iϕ Hψ ≡ H ′ ψ ′ In practice, the A k 's can have different physical meaning. They can represent the coupling to a true electromagnetic field, and in this case the replacement
div.formula: A k → q c A em k A 0 → - q A em 0
div.p: (q k being the particle charge) connects to the standard, non-relativistic, semiclassical theory of the dynamics of a spinless charge in an electromagnetic field. However, the A k 's can also represent the coupling to a pseudoelectromagnetic field, for instance that arising from the electronic subsystem in the exact factorization approach.
In this case, they read explicitly as
div.formula: A k = i u|∂ k u k = 0, 1, ..N
div.p: and are known collectively as Berry connection (for k = 1, 2, ..). Here, the |u ≡ |u(x) 's are local electronic states and the transformation properties of the connection reflect the (conjugate) U (1) action on these states that is needed to preserve the invariance of the total wavefunction, i.e., |u → |u ′ = e -iϕ |u .
In either case, it is expedient to introduce the differential 1-form
div.formula: ω = k A k dx k and its gauge-invariant exterior derivative dω = kj B kj dx k dx j ≡ B. A second gauge-invariant quantity is E = dA 0 -∂ t ω = k (∂ k A 0 -∂ t A k )dx k
div.p: to which we can add -dV if desired, without altering its gauge invariance. The magnetic and electric fields (either real or pseudo) are then described by the components of the above forms in the canonical basis {dx k }, i.e.,
div.formula: B kj = (∂ k A j -∂ j A k ) E k = (∂ k A 0 -∂ t A k ) (7)
div.p: and represent the "physical" (i.e., gauge invariant) effects that the gauge fields have on the particle dynamics. They satisfy two differential identities
div.formula: dB = 0 dE = -∂ t B(8)
div.p: that are, respectively, the magnetic Gauss and the Maxwell-Faraday induction law. They hold for both the real and the pseudo-electromagnetic fields and, in terms of components, read as
div.formula: ∂ i B jk + ∂ j B ki + ∂ k B ij = 0 ∂ k E j -∂ j E k = -∂ t B kj
div.p: In the following, we first introduce the key momentum and velocity fields, using their most general setting established by the above Hamiltonian. Then, we follow the historical route to quantum hydrodynamics by considering the problem of a single particle in three dimensional space, here subjected to a real or pseudo-electromagnetic field. The general result, which follows next, is a straightforward extension that could be directly derived from the Schrödinger equation, but the single particle problem is pivotal to identify its key physical ingredients. The established setting is instrumental to the analysis of the combined electron-nuclear dynamics in the exact factorization picture that is performed in Section III.

div.head: A. Momentum and velocity fields
div.p: The operator πk = -i ∂ k -A k entering Eq. 6 represents the k th component of the mechanical momentum, which connects to the velocity components vk via the mass tensor, namely through πk = j ξ kj vj where ξ kj is the mass tensor, j ξ kj ξ ji = δ i k , and vk = i [H, x k ] is the k th component of the velocity. Both πk and vk are gauge covariant, meaning that
div.formula: ψ → ψ ′ = e iϕ ψ ( Ĝψ) → ( Ĝ′ ψ ′ ) = e iϕ ( Ĝψ)
div.p: hence the expressions
div.formula: Π k = πk ψ ψ V k = vk ψ ψ
div.p: define two kinds of complex-valued, gauge-invariant fields in any point of configuration space but the ones where the wavefunction vanishes. They are, respectively, the momentum and the velocity fields and comprise qualitatively different contributions in their real and imaginary parts, henceforth denoted as π k , w k for the first and v k , u k for the second. By definition we have, for instance,
div.formula: Π k (x, t) = -i ∂ k ln ψ(x, t) -A k (x, t)(9)
div.p: and, using the polar representation of the wavefunction,
div.formula: π k (x, t) = ℜΠ k = (∂ k θ(x, t) -A k (x, t))(10)
w k (x, t) = ℑΠ k = -∂ k ln |ψ(x, t)| = -2 ∂ k ln n(x, t)(11
div.p: ) where θ = argψ and n = |ψ| 2 is the particle density in configuration space. Note the both π k and w k are illdefined (singular) when ψ = 0 [34]. Similarly, we have v k (x, t) = ℜV k = j ξ kj π j (x, t) and u k (x, t) = ℑV k = j ξ kj w j (x, t). The thus defined gauge-invariant, complex-valued fields Π k and V k (or the real-valued pairs (π k , w k ) and (v k , u k )), one for each k = 1, 2, ..N , replace the complexvalued wavefunction ψ (or its polar pair (|ψ|, θ)) in a redundant way, hence they satisfy some "consistency" conditions. These are more easily formulated for the momentum fields, because of their direct connection to ψ. A first relation concerns the imaginary parts w k 's which are longitudinal by construction, hence conservative in any connected domain,
div.formula: k ˆγ(a,b) w k dx k = -2 ln n(x b ) n(x a )
div.p: for any path joining the point x a with x b . This can be better expressed as
div.formula: n(x b ) = exp - 2 ˆγ(a,b) W n(x a )
div.p: where W is an exact 1-form W = k w k dx k = -/2d ln n and the path is arbitrary, subjected only to the constraint that it does not go through the wavefunction nodes or excluded region (if any). The velocity counterpart of this relation is similar but employs a different "metric". For a single particle in 3D we can write nu = -2m ∇n which can be viewed as a sort of Fick's law relating the density gradient to a kind of (number) density current on the left, with D = /2m playing the role of diffusion coefficient. For this reason u is termed osmotic velocity, a term that we shall adopt for u k , irrespective of the system dimensions (and nature of the mass tensor).

The real part of the momentum fields, π k , are trickier for at least two reasons. First of all, the definition of the fields includes the potentials A k which are essential for the gauge invariance and are usually non-longitudinal, thereby providing a "diamagnetic" contribution to the current. Secondly, but equally important, even when
div.formula: A k = 0 the momentum π k = (∂ k θ(x, t) -A k (x, t)
div.p: ) is only apparently longitudinal: the wavefunction phase θ is defined modulo 2π only, meaning that θ = argψ is not a smooth function on the whole complex plane, rather it presents a branch cut (typically placed on the real negative axis, in such a way that arg(C) = (-π, π]). That is, when selecting smoothly the phase θ along a loop there is no guarantee that it gets back to its original value, it may well change by an integer multiple of 2π. Accounting for this aspect we must have a quantization condition on the "circulation", namely
div.formula: Γ γ = k ˛γ(π k + A k )dx k = 2π n n ∈ Z(12)
div.p: for any closed path γ in configuration space. Here, n is the topological value that describes the way a loop winds around a singularity (or an excluded volume). In fact, n can be nonzero only in a multiply connected domain, since otherwise we could exploit Stokes' theorem to write
div.formula: Γ γ = ˆS dΠ + ˆS dω
div.p: where S is any open surface having γ as its frontier, dΠ = d( k π k dx k ) is the vorticity of the (mechanical) momentum field and the second integral is the B-flux through S (i.e., the Berry flux or the flux of the magnetic field, depending on the context). Stokes' theorem guarantees that the surface integral (hence, the circulation of Eq. 12) changes smoothly down to zero value when contracting the loop to a single point, thereby implying that n = 0 must hold for the finite loop as well.

We thus see that in the typical situation where there is no excluded volume the mechanical momentum field π k has a non-trivial vorticity only for a non-vanishing magnetic field, except at the singularities, i.e. at the wavefunction nodes.

Thus, a third issue worth mentioning here is the behavior of the momentum field around the wavefunction nodes [30]. Suppose x 0 is a node for the time evolving wavefunction ψ(x, t) at t = 0, i.e., ψ(x 0 , 0) = 0, and consider ψ(x 0 , δt) for infinitesimal times δt = ±|δt|. Unless ∂ t ψ(x 0 , 0) = 0 (a case that can be considered accidental) the wavefunction takes opposite values at opposite times ψ(x 0 , -|δt|) = -ψ(x 0 , +|δt|) and, by construction, passes through the origin of the complex plane at t = 0. Hence, its phase changes abruptly by π
div.formula: θ(x 0 , |δt|) = θ(x 0 , -|δt|) + π (mod2π)
div.p: Cleary, at t = 0 and x = x 0 the phase θ is completely undefined and the partial derivatives ∂ t (π k + A k ) are singular. Note that, in general, the wavefunction nodes are objects of codimension 2 as they are found at the intersection of two surfaces, i.e. those defined by the conditions ℜψ(x) = ℑψ(x) = 0.
B. QHD for a particle in 3D space with gauge fields
We start with the simple example of a particle of mass m in 3D space, namely with the Hamiltonian

that may represent a charge of mass m subjected to a true electromagnetic field, provided the gauge potentials are correctly scaled for the particle charge. As mentioned above, there is no real difficulty in dealing directly with the general case of Eq. 6, but this single-particle example allows us to single out the key physical ingredients that are otherwise hard to identify in the general setting. We only sketch the derivations and present the main (textbook) results [29,30], though we put some emphasis on the gauge invariance of the theory which is crucial for the issues discussed in the next Section.
In this problem, the momentum and velocity fields are simply related to each other, and we choose to work with the velocity field
div.formula: V = v + iu where v = m (∇θ -A) and u = -2m ∇ ln n = -m ∇ ln R, with R = √ n = |ψ|. From the Schrödinger equation in "local" form i ∂ t ln ψ = ψ * Hψ n
div.p: (which is valid everywhere in configuration space but the wavefunction nodes) one obtains the set of coupled (real) equations for the pair of variables (n, θ) that represent the wavefunction in polar form. These equations require a complex-valued "energy density" that, for H above, reads as
div.formula: ψ * Hψ ≡ m 2 n V * V + n(V -A 0 ) -i 2 ∇(nV)(13)
div.p: They are, respectively, the celebrated continuity equation for the particle density [35]
and a kind of Hamilton-Jacobi equation for the "action"
which involves the quantum potential
Eq. 15 represents the key dynamical equation of the De Broglie -Bohm approach, corresponding [37] to the particle equation of motion
div.formula: x(t 0 ) = x 0 v(t 0 ) = v 0 = 1 m (∇S(x 0 , t 0 ) -A(x 0 , t 0 )) m v = E + v × B -∇(V + Q) where v = ẋ, E = (∇A 0 -∂ t A)
div.p: is the electric field and B = ∇ × ( A) is the pseudo-vector describing the magnetic field. Solution of this equation for any initial position x 0 (and corresponding v 0 , as determined by the initial wavefunction) provides in principle a solution of the quantum dynamical problem -i.e. the wavefunction at later times. However, note that on comparing with the classical Hamilton-Jacobi equation, the added term
div.formula: Q = Q(n(x, t)
div.p: ) is a potential that depends on the particle density, and this is the crucial factor that makes the system quantum. The trajectory equation does determine (as in classical mechanics) the evolution of a classical-like system but now the trajectories are quantum trajectories whose (changing) density affects the equation of motion itself. This implies that it is not possible to solve for a single or a handful of trajectories, unless we know in advance the evolving density n(x, t).

Were it not for the classical analogy, the phase equation of the De Broglie -Bohm approach, Eq. 15, would be an ordinary partial differential equation, not easier to solve than the Schrödinger equation we started from. Importantly, this equation involves yet the potentials, that are gauge dependent, and thus it is not in a gauge invariant form. In fact, θ(x, t) = S(x, t)/ itself is gauge dependent. In order to fix this problem and to find the (gauge invariant) particle equations of motion one can take the spatial derivative of the Schrödinger equation, to write
div.formula: m ∂V ∂t = - ∂A ∂t -∇ Hψ ψ(17)
div.p: where the r.h.s. can be obtained from the energy density of Eq. 13. The result is the "Lagrangian-frame" expression
div.formula: m dv dt = E+v × B-∇ (V + Q)(18)
div.p: where
is the material derivative [38] and E and B are, respectively, the pseudo-electric and the pseudo-magnetic fields introduced above. This equation needs to be complemented with continuity equation,
div.formula: dn dt + n∇v = 0(20)
div.p: (here written in Lagrangian form, too) and, together, the two represent Madelung's hydrodynamical equations of motion. The solutions of these equations solve the original quantum dynamical problem if one enforces the quantization condition on the circulation, namely Eq. 12. This is necessary to guarantee the existence of a scalar function S ≡ θ that is independent of the path (modulo 2π ). This quantization condition (a boundary condition for the velocity field) is required at initial time only, since Kelvin's circulation theorem [39,40] -i.e., Γγ = 0 for any closed curve γ in motion with the fluid elements -also applies to the fluid dynamics described by Eqs. 18 and 20 (see Appendix A).
Eqs. 18 and 20 can be further presented as a set of coupled equations for the real (v) and imaginary (u) velocity fields, namely
div.formula: m du dt = 2 ∇(∇v) -m(u∇)v + u × B m dv dt = F + m(u∇)u -2 ∇ 2 u (21
div.p: where F = E+v × B -∇V represents the total (classical ) force acting on the particle. This set of equations describes a particle dynamics subjected to the classical force F and "an internal force" (second and third term on the r.h.s. of the last equation) that depends on the auxiliary field u. The latter evolves along the flow as dictated by the first equation, and replaces the quantum potential appearing in the De Broglie -Bohm formulation [41]. Noteworthy, Eq.s 21 can be combined into a single equation for the complex-valued fields,
div.formula: m dV dt = F -i 2m ∇ × B + i 2 ∇ 2 V (22
div.p: where now F = E+V × B -∇V is a complex-valued classical force and

is the complex-valued material derivative, both involving the complex-valued velocity field V in place of the real one. This result follows more easily by introducing the complex-valued action F (x) according to
div.formula: ψ = exp i F(x)
div.p: (i.e., setting F (x) = S(x) -i 2 ln n(x)) and writing a complex-valued Hamiltonian-Jacobi equation for it. However, in practice, the first (vectorial) equation of Eq. 21 is conveniently replaced by the continuity equation for the scalar n, at the expense of breaking the symmetry between velocity fields, hence Eq. 22 becomes just a compact way of presenting the QHD equations of motion.
Finally, we notice that the key equation of motion, Eq. 18, can be recast as a momentum balance equationi.e. as a quantum Navier-Stokes equation -
div.formula: m n dv dt = n F b + ∇σ(24)
div.p: where the bulk force F b is classical (F b ≡ F) and the surface force is quantum. The latter reads componentwise as (∇σ) k = j ∂ j σ jk , and is determined by the quantum stress tensor
div.formula: σ jk = -δ jk P q -m nu j u k (25
div.p: where P q = -2 4m ∇ 2 n is the (positive definite) quantum pressure. The analogy with the classical Navier-Stokes, though, can only be formal. The fluid of quantum probability addressed here does not experience any frictional effect since this would require an interaction between fluid molecules, one that can transfer momentum when the molecules travel at different speeds. Here the interaction is a manifestation of the non-locality of quantum mechanics, it depends on the density gradient and it is same irrespective of whether or not the "particles" travel at the same velocity.

div.head: C. Many-particle QHD with gauge fields
div.p: We are now ready to undertake the more complicated task of developing the hydrodynamic picture for the many-body Hamiltonian of Eq. 6. We shall use the complex valued momentum fields introduced in Sec. II A, Eq. 9, and the result
div.formula: Hψ ψ = ij ξ ij 2 Π * i Π j -i 2 j ∂ j (nV j ) n + V -A 0 (26)
div.p: Eq. 26 defines the potential of the complex Eulerian force
div.formula: ∂Π k ∂t = - ∂A k ∂t -∂ k Hψ ψ(27)
div.p: that shows that non-longitudinal contributions to the momentum field can only come from the timedependence of the vector potential A k , consistently with the generalized Maxwell-Faraday induction law of Eq. 8.
There remains to calculate the spatial derivative of Eq. 26. A simple calculation gives for the non-trivial kinetic contribution
div.formula: ∂ k T ψ ψ = -i 2 j ∂ k ∂ j V j + j V j (∂ k Π j )(28)
div.p: that can be further rearranged as
div.formula: ∂ k T ψ ψ = -i 2 ij ξ ij ∂ j ∂ i Π k + i 2 ij ξ ij ∂ j B ki (29) + j V j (∂ j Π k ) - j V j B kj(30)
div.p: where we have used
div.formula: ∂ k Π j -∂ j Π k = -(∂ k A j -∂ j A k ) = -B kj .
div.p: Adding the potential contribution and rearranging, we finally find
div.formula: d Π k dt = Fk -i 2 ij ξ ij ∂ j B ki + i 2 ij ξ ij ∂ i ∂ j Π k (31)
div.p: where we have introduced the complex material derivative
div.formula: d dt = ∂ ∂t + j V j ∂ j (32
div.p: and the complex-valued total classical force,
div.formula: Fk = E k + j V j B kj -∂ k V(33)
div.p: Eq. 31 generalizes Eq. 22 for the single particle in 3D space, here re-written in terms of the momentum field,
div.formula: d Π dt = F -i 2m ∇ × B + i 2m ∇ 2 Π
div.p: where in 3D the pseudo-vector field B is related to the tensor B kj by B i = 1 2 kj ǫ ikj B kj (here ǫ ijk is the Levi-Civita symbol that stands for the fully antisymmetric unit tensor, ǫ 123 = 1).
Eq. 31 is the complex-valued form of Madelung's hydrodynamic equations. It can be easily split into a pair of real-valued equations by taking its real and imaginary parts. Upon using
div.formula: 2 ij ξ ij ∂ j (∂ i π k -B ki ) = 2 ij ξ ij ∂ j ∂ k π i ≡ 2 j ∂ k ∂ j v j , we find dπ k dt = F k + j u j ∂ j w k -2 ij ξ ij ∂ i ∂ j w k(34)
div.p: and
div.formula: dw k dt = 2 j ∂ k ∂ j v j - j u j ∂ j π k + j u j B kj (35)
div.p: which generalize Eqs. 21, here re-written in terms of momentum fields,
div.formula: dπ dt = F + (u∇)w -2m ∇ 2 w dw dt = 2 ∇(∇v) -(u∇)π + u × B
div.p: Eq. 35 is, of course, continuity equation in disguise. Hence, at the expense of breaking the symmetry between real and imaginary momentum fields, it is conveniently replaced by
or, equivalently, by its Lagrangian form
div.formula: dn dt + n j ∂ j v j = 0(37)
div.p: Eqs. 37 and 34 form the basis for the trajectory approach to quantum dynamics underlying the Madelung hydrodynamic picture. Eq. 34 shows that, in analogy with the single particle case, the dynamics is governed by the classical force (here comprising both "external" and gauge fields) and by a quantum force
div.formula: (F q ) k = j u j ∂ j w k -2 ij ξ ij ∂ i ∂ j w k (38
div.p: depending only on the density. The latter can be rearranged to identify the quantum potential
div.formula: Q Q = - 2 2 ij ξ ij ∂ i ∂ j R R(39)
div.p: an expression that closely parallels that found above for the single particle in 3D space, Eq. 16. The analogy can be tightened if we introduce a mass-weighted Laplacian according to
div.formula: ∆ M := ij ξ ij ∂ i ∂ j(40)
div.p: and rewrite Eqs. 39 and 16 in the suggestive form
The mass-weighted Laplacian is further in accordance with other analogies provided above in the equations of motion, e.g. it can be used to write the momentum equation as
div.formula: dπ k dt = F k + j u j ∂ j w k -2 ∆ M w k (43
div.p: in close analogy with Eq. 21, or that for the complexvalued field
div.formula: d Π k dt = Fk -i 2 ij ξ ij ∂ j B ki + i 2 ∆ M Π k(44)
div.p: (cf. with Eq. 22).

The resulting quantum potential can also be used to obtain the density of quantum force which enters the momentum balance equation (i.e. the Navier-Stokes equation for the momentum) in the form of divergence of a stress tensor
div.formula: n dπ k dt = nF k + j ∂ j σ j k (45)
div.p: with the quantum stress tensor given by
div.formula: σ j k = -δ j k P q -nu j w k (46)
div.p: and the quantum pressure P q = -2 4 ∆ M n. Eq. 46 parallels (generalizes) Eq. 25 found for the particle in 3D space. Notice that u j is the j th imaginary component of the velocity, while w k is the k th imaginary component of the mechanical momentum.

div.head: D. Statistical mixtures
div.p: The theory presented so far has only addressed the case of a pure state, for which a set of two hydrodynamic equations suffices. These can be formulated as the momentum particle equation augmented with the continuity equation or as two Euler equations, one for the density (continuity equation) and one for the momentum density. For (proper) mixtures of states an analogous and yet very elegant description is possible in terms of momentum moments of the underlying phase-space (pseudo) distribution [42][43][44], which generalize the density and the momentum density appearing in the pure state problem to higher order quantities. The main difference between a mixed and a pure state is that the coupled equations of motion in general do not close at the first order level (i.e., at the level of the momentum density), rather they form an infinite set, arranged in a hierarchical form, that in practice must be closed somehow. It is only for a pure state that the second (and higher) order moment can be expressed in terms of the zero-th and the first moments only, although in a non-trivial way [45][46].

The standard construction of momentum moments and of the related hierarchy of equations of motion is well known. It is however of limited help here since the presence of gauge potentials in our Hamiltonian, Eq. 6, makes the ordinary momentum moments gauge dependent, hence unsuited for a gauge invariant hydrodynamic description of the quantum dynamics. Thus, we introduce here a new type of gauge invariant momentswhich we call mechanical momentum moments (MMMs) because of their relationship with the mechanical momentum operator -and show that their equations of motion take a simple, physically sound form. In the following we provide the main results of these developments, the details of the calculations can be found in Appendix B.
The ordinary momentum moments are the moments of the partial "distributions" that are obtained when fixing q in the Wigner function ρ W (q, p), namely
div.formula: S (n) i1i2..iD (q) = ˆdD p p i1 1 p i2 2 ..p iD D ρ W (q, p) defines the i 1 i 2 ..i D component of the moment of order n = D k i k , D
div.p: is the number of degrees of freedom and ρ W (q, p) is the Wigner function
div.formula: ρ W (q, p) = ˆdD r (2π ) D σ q - r 2
div.p: , q + r 2 e i pr associated to the system density matrix σ(x, x ′ ). For instance, in this notation, S (0) 00..0 (q) is just the density in configuration space, S
(1) 01..0 (q) is the second component of the first order moment, etc.
There exist several equivalent ways to express these moments, the one that is most useful for our purposes is by means of
div.formula: P (n) ijk.. (q) = q|[p i , [p j , [p k , ..σ] + ] + ] + |q 2 n
div.p: where [A, B] + = AB + BA is the anti-commutator and now the hat denotes abstract operators, e.g, pk and σ for the k th momentum and for the density operator, respectively. In the last expression, the subscript labels the coordinates and specifies the order of the products -the momentum operators appearing in the nested products are labeled with indexes from right to left, starting from the innermost product. However, since the pk 's commute with each other, the P (n) ijk.. (q) 's so defined are fully symmetric in their indexes and coincide with the moments above. That is,
div.formula: P (n) ijk.. (q) = P (n) ikj.. (q) = P (n) kij.. (q).. ≡ S (n) i1i2..iD i m = count([ijk..], m)
div.p: where count([ijk..], m) counts the occurrences of m in the list of indexes ijk... This representation of the moments is redundant but it is very useful if we intend to generalize the moment construction to gauge covariant, noncommuting operators. Specifically, in connection with the dynamics provided by our Hamiltonian, Eq. 6, we define mechanical momentum moments according to
This definition can be equivalently expressed in a form that is rather useful for calculations, namely
div.formula: M (n) ijk.. (q) = πi+π ′ * i 2 πj +π ′ * j 2 π k +π ′ * k 2 .. σ(x, x ′ )| x=x ′ =q(48)
div.p: where now
div.formula: π k = -i ∂ k -A k , π ′ k = -i ∂ ′ k -A ′ k and A k ≡ A k (x), A ′ k ≡ A k (x ′
div.p: ) (see Appendix B for details). Note that here the symbol π k , differently from the previous Sections, is used to mean a differential operator.
The moments defined in this way are, by construction, gauge invariant and they thus fulfill our requirement. The price to be paid is that they are no longer symmetric in their indexes since
div.formula: [π j + π ′ * j , π k + π ′ * k ] = [π j , π k ] + [π ′ * j , π ′ * k ] ≡ i (B jk -B ′ jk )
div.p: where again we have primed (unprimed) quantities for functions of x ′ (x). For instance, a direct calculation shows that
div.formula: M (3) ijk -M (3) ikj = - i 2 2 ∂ i B jk (q) M (0) (q) M (4) mijk -M (4) mikj = - i 2 2 ∂ i B jk (q) M (1) m (q)+ +∂ m B jk (q) M (1) i
div.p: The exception is the symmetry in the two leftmost indexes
div.formula: M (n) ijk.. (q) = M (n) jik.. (q)
div.p: which holds for any moment of order n ≥ 2.
Note that we could define moments which are yet gauge invariant and symmetric in their indexes, for instance
div.formula: N (n) ijk.. (q) = p i + p ′ * i 2 -A i (q) p j + p ′ * j 2 -A j (q) × p k + p ′ * k 2 -A k (q) .. σ(x, x ′ )| x=x ′ =q
div.p: However, only for the M (n) ijk.. 's defined above the equations of motion take a simple form.
The moment equations of motion follow indeed from the Liouville -von Neumann (LvN) equation and for the first three moments take the form (see Appendix B)
div.formula: + ij ξ ij B ki (q)M(1)
j (q) ∂M (2) km ∂t (q) = - ij ξ ij ∂ i M(3)
kmj (q)+ + (-∂ k V + E k ) M (1) m (q)+ + (-∂ m V + E m ) M (1) k (q)+ + ij ξ ij B ki (q)M (2) mj (q) + B mi (q)M (2) kj (q) (51)
div.p: In general, for the n th moment we have an "upward" coupling to the (n + 1) th layer of moments that occurs through a surface term containing the spatial derivatives of the (n+1) th moments, and both "downward" and "horizontal" couplings that occur through "classical" forces. The rank-1 pseudo-electric field E k and the intrinsic force -∂ k V link the n th layer of moments to the layer below it. The rank-2 pseudo-magnetic field B kj increases the order by one and thus describes "horizontal" coupling to moments of the same order n (starting from n = 1).
In the above hydrodynamic set of equations the first, Eq. 49, is continuity equation, with
div.formula: J i (q) = j ξ ij M (1)
div.p: j (q) playing the role of density current. The second equation, Eq. 50, extends the quantum Navier-Stokes equation for a pure state, Eq. 45,
div.formula: n dπ :k dt = nF k + j ∂ j σ j k
div.p: here written in the form
div.formula: ∂ t (nπ k ) = nF k - j ∂ j (nv j π k ) + j ∂ j σ j k
div.p: where use has been made of the identity ∂ t (nπ k ) + j ∂ j (nv j π k ) ≡ n dπ k /dt. The comparison with the pure state is insightful, since it suggests to single out the "uncorrelated" term from the second moment by defining the second cumulant as
For a pure state we have
div.formula: - ij ξ ij ∂ i C kj = i ∂ i σ i k ≡ F S k
div.p: and the cumulant is seen to be the source of the density of surface forces, F S k (the only ones having a quantum origin). In general, i.e. for an arbitrary mixed state, we can always introduce the rank-2 tensor C
(2) kl (q) according to Eq. 52 and re-write Eq. 50 in terms of it. The result is
div.formula: dπ k dt (q) = - 1 M (0) (q) ij ξ ij ∂ i C (2) kj (q)+ + (-∂ k V(q) + E k ) + j B kj (q)v j (q)(53)
div.p: where
div.formula: π k = M(1)
div.p: k /M (0) and v j = i ξ ji π i are the usual momentum and velocity fields. We thus see that, in the form of Eq. 53, the first moment equation describes the Lagrangian-frame evolution of momentum in terms of classical, bulk forces and a surface term which is responsible for quantum effects.

div.head: III. QUANTUM HYDRODYNAMICS OF EXACTLY FACTORIZED ELECTRONS-NUCLEI
div.p: We are now ready to address the main issue of this paper, that is replacing the EF equations with gauge invariant equations of motion for both electrons and nuclei. We shall start with the pure state case where this program can be accomplished in an exact way, and later we shall explore extensions of the theory to special statistical mixtures that require some assumptions.

div.head: A. Pure states
div.p: We recall that the generic Hamiltonian of Eq. 6, is also the Hamiltonian governing the time evolution of the marginal probability amplitude describing the nuclei in the exact factorization approach to the electron-nuclear problem. Hence, as shown in the previous Section this immediately leads to a gauge invariant formulation of the nuclear dynamics in hydrodynamical form, for instance that provided in Lagrangian frame by the complex momentum equation presented in Eq. 44, with the complex momentum fields Π k given by Eq. 32. In that case, the dynamical problem needs to be augmented by the coupled equation of motion for the local electronic states, Eq. 4,
div.formula: i Q   ∂ ∂t + j V j ∂ j   |u = QH el |u -R |u(54)
div.p: here written for the only relevant component of ∂ t |uthe gauge covariant projection Q∂ t |u which is "free" of the gauge choice -and rearranged to make explicit on the l.h.s. the complex material derivative of Eq. 23. This equation can be presented in complex hydrodynamical form the closely parallels that momentum equation for the nuclei (Eq. 44), i.e., in the form
div.formula: i Q d dt |u = Q H el |u - 2 2 ∆M |u
div.p: where ∆M := ij ξ ij D i D j is a mass-weighted gauge covariant Laplacian (cf. with Eq. 40), defined with the help of the gauge covariant derivative D j = ∂ j + iA j . Alternatively, one can work with real-valued nuclear fields and consider
div.formula: i Q d |u dt = QH el |u + j u j Q∂ j |u -R |u(55)
div.p: in parallel to the real-valued momentum equation for the nuclei, Eq. 43, and jointly with the continuity equation (Eq. 37). Upon making explicit the role of the density gradients, one can present the dynamical equations in a suggestive form, namely
div.formula: iQ d |ϕ dt = Q   |Φ -2 ij ξ ij (∂ i ln n)∂ j |ϕ -2 ∆M |ϕ  (56
div.p: ) for the electrons and
div.formula: dπ k dt =   F k -2 ij ξ ij (∂ i ln n)∂ j w k -2 ∆ M w k  (57)
div.p: for the nuclei, where now |ϕ = |u and |Φ = H el |u / . For k = 1, 2, ..D Eq. 57 represents trajectory equations similar to those of the De Broglie -Bohm -Madelung theory, and defines the evolution of the elementary volumes of the quantum probability fluid in nuclear configuration space (noting that the second and third terms in Eq. 57 are just the quantum force, see Eq. 38). However, they are now augmented by an equation for the electronic state, Eq. 56, that follows each fluid element
div.formula: |ϕ ≡ |u(x t , t)
div.p: similarly to the quantum hydrodynamical description of a particle with a spin [29]. This "quantum vector field" associated to the nuclear fluid evolves according to the local electronic Hamiltonian (first term on the r.h.s. of Eq. 56) and the coupling to the nuclear motion (second and third term on the r.h.s. of Eq. 56). The latter takes a form which is remarkably similar to the quantum force contribution appearing in the quantum trajectory of motion for the nuclei: they share precisely the same coupling factor (density dependent) and differ only in the auxiliary field they couple to, which is |ϕ for the electronic variables and the set of w k 's for the nuclear variables [47].

In the above equations the effect of the electron dynamics on the nuclear motion is condensed in the "classical" force F k (the real part of Eq. 33), containing both the averaged electronic forces and the gauge fields,
div.formula: F k = -∂ k V + j v j B kj + (∂ k A 0 -∂ t A k )(58)
div.p: Here, three distinct contributions are evident. The first term is the scalar potential known from the Born-Oppenheimer approximation, though now involving a time dependent electronic state. It explicitly reads as
div.formula: V = E el + φ FS = u|H el |u + 2 ij ξ ij 2 g ij
div.p: where E el = E el (x) is the average (local) electronic energy, g ij = ℜq kj is the Fubini-Study metric tensor and q ij = ∂ i u|Q|∂ j u the instantaneous quantum geometric tensor. This is the term that was dubbed "gaugeinvariant part of the time-dependent potential energy surface" in the seminal work by Gross and coworkers on exact factorization [48]. The second term of Eq. 58 represents the usual pseudo-magnetic force driven by the Berry curvature B kj = -2 ℑq kj while the last term of Eq. 58 is the "electron dynamical" force, the only one containing the time-derivative of the electronic state,
div.formula: F ED k ≡ -2 ℑ ∂ k u|Q∂ t u .
div.p: This last contribution precisely corresponds to the pseudo-electric field E k , as defined in Eq. 7. However, in the context of the exact factorization of the wavefunction, it would be misleading to label it as "electric" due to the presence of a velocitydependent (magnetic) contribution. Indeed, upon making apparent the material derivative of the electronic state, we find
div.formula: F ED k = -2 ℑ ∂ k u|Q| u - j v j B kj = G ED k - j v j B kj(59
div.p: ) where G ED k -defined by this equation -is the "Lagrangian-frame" electron-dynamical force. G ED k is free of velocity contributions, as a simple inspection of Eq. 55 reveals. Hence, the total classical force of Eq. 58 does not explicitly depend on the nuclear velocity,
div.formula: F k = -∂ k V + G ED
div.p: Furthermore, upon using Eq. 55, G ED k can be split as
div.formula: G ED k = δF BO k + δF el k (60
div.p: where, δF BO
div.formula: δF el k = 2 j u j g kj -2 ℜ ∂ k u|R|u(61)
div.p: is the corresponding correction to the pseudo-electric force -∂ k φ FS appearing already in the adiabatic approximation. Since the dynamically corrected BO and electric forces read as, respectively,
div.formula: F BO k := F BO k + δF BO k ≡ -u|∂ k H el |u(62)
div.p: and [49]
div.formula: F el k := -∂ k φ FS + δF el k ≡ - 2 n ij ξ ij ∂ i (ng kj )(63)
div.p: we arrive at
div.formula: F k = -u|∂ k H el |u - 2 n ij ξ ij ∂ i (ng kj )(64)
div.p: This is the same result as obtained in our previous work [27,28], if it were not for the fact that here the pseudomagnetic force vanishes precisely (and not on average)

[50]. The reason is that we are now dealing with the classical force (as needed in the hydrodynamic picture) and not with the quantum one used in Refs. [27,28], i.e., that in the Heisenberg picture [51]. For comparison, Table I gives the full set of Heisenberg-picture force components for both an adiabatic and an exact dynamics.
A few comments are in order at this point. First of all, F el k (Eq. 63) is manifestly zero-averaged when interpreted as (local) operator acting on the nuclear wavefunction
div.formula: ˆdxψ * F el,c k ψ = -2 ij ξ ij ˆdx∂ i (ng kj ) ≡ 0
div.p: but it is generally non-zero as a classical force, i.e. as required by the trajectory equation, Eq. 57. Furthermore, as already observed in Refs. [27,28], the dynamical correction to the BO force turns the force of the electronically-averaged potential into a mean electronic force: this is a key difference since the first is manifestly conservative while the latter is not (unless the electronic state is stationary). The same occurs for the pseudo-electric (Fubini-Study) force, which is turned non-conservative when dynamically corrected. Hence, in a sense, the electron dynamics works as an electromotive force for the nuclear dynamics. The equations of motion (Eqs. 56,57) are not yet in the desired form, since the electronic equation ( 56) is gauge covariant rather than gauge invariant. To bring it in the required form we focus on the conditional electronic density operator ρ el = |u u| ≡ ρ el (x) and on its dynamics, as it follows from Eq. 55. As shown in detail in Appendix C, this is a closed equation for the density operator, involving ρ el itself and its spatial first and second derivatives. We emphasize that the ρ el (x)'s are conditional operators, and that they are only loosely connected to the true electronic density operator γ el , hence the existence and knowledge of an electronic equation for the ρ el (x)'s are not obvious. In particular, we remind that γ el is the convex sum of the ρ el 's over nuclear configuration space, weighted by the nuclear density (see Ref. [28], Sec. IIIC),
div.formula: γ el = ˆdxn(x)ρ el (x)(65)
div.p: The Liouville-von Neumann-like equation of motion governing the evolution of ρ el reads as (see Appendix C)
div.formula: i ρel = [H el + δH en , ρ el ](66)
div.p: where the dot stands for the material derivative and δH en is the electron-nuclear correction to the electronic Hamiltonian defining the Liouvillian
div.formula: δH en = - 2 2n ij ξ ij ∂ i (n∂ j ρ el ),(67)
div.p: Eq. 66 is both trace-and purity-conserving, i.e.,
Tr el ρel = 0 Tr el (ρ el ρel ) = 0 see Appendix C for a proof [52]. Jointly with the continuity equation for the nuclear density and with the momentum equation for the nuclei,
div.formula: dπ k dt = -u|∂ k H el |u - 2 n ij ξ ij ∂ i (ng kj )+ + 2 4n ij ξ ij ∂ i (n∂ j ∂ k ln n)(68)
div.p: it forms a full set of gauge invariant equations for both the electrons and the nuclei. Here, in re-writing Eq. 57 we have made explicit the role of the electronic Hamiltonian and that of the gauge fields, and we have combined the quantum contributions in a single term. Eqs. 66 and 68 represent the first main finding of this work: they provide an exact, gauge invariant description of the electronic and nuclear dynamics, in the Lagrangian frame defined by the nuclear quantum fluid. Alternatively, the nuclear hydrodynamical equations of motion can be given in the Eulerian frame. Using the moment notation and the results of Section II D, they read as
div.formula: ∂M (0) ∂t (q) = - ij ξ ij ∂ i M (1) j (q) (69) ∂M (1) k ∂t (q) = - ij ξ ij ∂ i M(2)
div.p: for the zero-th and the first moment, respectively, and replace Eqs. 68 and 37 formulated in Lagrangian frame.
Here, the net force F k is (see Eq. 64)
div.formula: F k = -Tr el (ρ el ∂ k H el ) - 2 M (0) ij ξ ij ∂ i (M (0) g kj ) (71)
div.p: and the second moment is subjected to the pure-state closure

Before closing this Section we emphasize that one main advantage of a gauge invariant formulation of the dynamics is that it removes the need of obtaining the electronic wavefunctions with a smooth phase factor, when the latter is computed on-the-fly along the nuclear trajectories. This issue can pose significant difficulties, especially in systems with broken time-reversal invariance-e.g. in the presence of a magnetic field. In such cases, the overlap between adjacent electronic states, u(x t )|u(x t+∆t ) , takes arbitrary phases and it becomes challenging to make it smooth by selecting an appropriate phase from
div.formula: Adiabatic Exact Exact BOI -∂ k u0|H el |u0 -∂ k u|H el |u -u|∂ k H el |u elI - 2 2 ξ ij ∂ k g 0 ij - 2 2 ξ ij ∂ k gij - 2 n ξ ij ∂i(ng jk ) magI 2 j (v 0 j B 0 kj + B kj v0 j ) 2 j (vj B kj + B kj vj ) 2 j (∆vjB 0 kj + B kj ∆vj ) EDI -2 ℑ ∂ k u|Q|∂tu
div.p: Table I. Quantum mechanical (Heisenberg) forces [BO = Born-Oppenheimer, el = pseudo-electric, mag = pseudo-magnetic, ED = electrodynamical] acting on the nuclei in both the adiabatic approximation (second column) and the exact dynamics, with either the electron dynamical force (third column) or the dynamical corrections (fourth columns). The superscript 0 indicates the use of a stationary-state connection and metric, n = |ψ| 2 is the nuclear density and ∆vj = vj -ℜV j . The forces are all local except for the pseudo-magnetic one. As a consequence, only the latter needs to be "classicalized" in the hydrodynamical picture, with the replacement vj → v j . This procedure makes the dynamically corrected pseudo-magnetic force identically vanishing in the hydrodynamic picture.

the random values generated by the electronic solver. In the above formulation, on the other hand, the only information required consists of the gauge invariant fields, and these can be directly obtained from ρ el and its spatial derivatives. For instance,
div.formula: q kj = Tr el (ρ el (∂ k ρ el ) (∂ j ρ el ))(73)
div.p: provides the instantaneous quantum geometric tensor, hence the Fubini-Study metric (which is needed for the dynamics, see the force of Eq. 71) and the Berry curvature. Furthermore, we notice that upon multiplying Eq. 66 by n(x) and integrating over all nuclear configuration space one obtains
div.formula: ∂γ el ∂t = - i ˆdx n(x) [H el (x), ρ el (x)](74)
div.p: which gives the evolution of the "true" density operator of the electronic subsystem -that is, γ el of Eq. 65in terms of local (i.e., conditional) contributions. This equation is an exact identity for globally pure states that can be generalized to the statistical mixtures discussed below. It shows that out of the two terms on the r.h.s. of Eq. 66 only the first one (the Liouvillian associated to H el ) contributes directly to the change of the global electronic properties, the second one (i.e. the electronnuclear coupling δH en ) affects the local properties (the ρ el 's) that in turn determine a change in γ el . In a sense, Eq. 74 proves that H el plays a primary role in the evolution of the electronic properties. We should emphasize, though, that this result is of limited help in general since most often the observables of interest are electron-nuclear rather than purely electronic, and thus require more detailed information than those contained in γ el . A notable example consists in the adiabatic populations p ad n (apparently, pure electronic observables) which are defined as
div.formula: p ad n = ˆdxn(x)Tr(ρ el P n )
div.p: where P n (x) is the eigenprojector on the n th eigenstate of H el . These are, in fact, expectation values of the following electron-nuclear operator
div.formula: P ad n = ˆdx |x P n (x) x|
div.p: and always require n(x) and the conditional density operators ρ el , unless the relevant eigenspace is trivial and P n (x) does not depend on x.

div.head: B. Type-n statistical mixtures
div.p: In Section III A we have provided an exact, gaugeinvariant description of the dynamics using the exact factorization of the wavefunction describing the globally pure state of the combined electronic-nuclear system. We now consider extensions to this picture, starting from the situation where the electron-nuclear system is in a simple, proper statistical mixture of states where electrons can be yet described by a local pure electronic state. By this we mean that the nuclear-coordinate representation of the total density operator changes according to [53]
where σ(x, x ′ ) -the apparent nuclear density matrix -is no longer constrained to have a pure state form. We call this special mixture of states a type-n statistical mixture.

In this case, the equations of motion for the nuclei and for the electrons follow easily as a simple generalization of the equations given in the Section II D and III A. Specifically, electrons are governed by the same equations we have seen above, namely Eq. 66 for the Lagrangianframe dynamics of the conditional electronic density operators. We only need to perform an ensemble average of the complex-valued nuclear velocity or momentum fields appearing there, specifically in the definition of the material derivative on the l.h.s. of Eq. 66 and in the e-n correction term on its r.h.s. (Eq. 67). With nuclei described by the mixed-state apparent density matrix σ(x, x ′ ) the real component of the momentum field takes the form of a ratio of mechanical momentum moments
div.formula: π k (q) = M (1) k (q) M (0) (q) since J i (q) = j ξ ij M (1)
div.p: j (q) is the correct expression for the nuclear current density. As for the imaginary component we need a similar construction, that translates into a logarithmic average n eff of the nuclear density entering the electronic equation, namely
div.formula: ln n eff (q) = α ω α ln n α (q)
div.p: where ω α are statistical weights and n α (q) = ψ * α (q)ψ α (q) is the density of the α th representative of the ensemble. Here, we can replace n eff with M (0) , which is logarithmically correct up to second order in the (relative) density fluctuations in the ensemble
div.formula: n eff ≈ M (0) exp - δn 2 (M (0) ) 2
div.p: to write the electron-nuclear correction term as in the pure state case,
div.formula: δ Hen = - 2 2M (0) ij ξ ij ∂ i (M (0) ∂ j ρ el )
div.p: with the ensemble average nuclear density M (0) in place of ψ * ψ and ρ el the usual conditional density operator for the electrons.

As for the nuclei, the dynamical behavior of any realization of the ensemble is governed by one and the same Hamiltonian H eff , with the gauge fields coming from the evolution of the (local) pure electronic state. As a result, the nuclear dynamics can be described by a Liouvillevon Neumann equation for the density matrix σ(x, x ′ ) of the kind already mentioned in Sec. II D, meaning that it can be given as a hierarchy of momentum moment equations
div.formula: ∂M (0) ∂t (q) = - ij ξ ij ∂ i M (1) j (q) ∂M (1) k ∂t (q) = - ij ξ ij ∂ i M (2) kj (q) + F k M (0) (q) ∂M (2) km ∂t (q) = - ij ξ ij ∂ i M (3) kmj (q)+ + F k M (1) m (q) + F m M(1)
k (q)+ + ij ξ ij B ki C (2) mj (q) + B mi C (2) kj (q)
div.p: ... where again (in the approximation n eff ≈ M (0) ) the net force F k is given by Eq. 71 and C
(2) kj (q) was defined in Eq. 52.

Hence, in a type-n statistical mixture the electronic equation of motion takes precisely the same form of the pure state case, Eq. 66, and the hierarchy of equations for the nuclear moments generalizes the pure state result of Eqs. 69 and 70, following the general scheme presented in Section II D. We notice that in the latter set of equations cancellation of the gauge fields is incomplete beyond the first moment: coupling to the magnetic field occurs through the first-moment correlations, which are generally non-vanishing beyond the first moment.

div.head: C. Type-e statistical mixtures
div.p: We now consider the opposite limit of a statistical mixture where the nuclei can be yet represented by a wavefunction ψ(x) and the electrons are in an mixed state, meaning that even locally in nuclear coordinates they are not constrained to have a pure state form. In other words, in this case the total density operator takes the form
where Ŵ(x, x ′ ) is the ensemble average of the pure-state dyad Ŵα (x,
div.formula: x ′ ) = |u α (x) u α (x ′ )|, Ŵ(x, x ′ ) = α ω α Ŵα (x, x ′ )
div.p: with ω α statistical weights, α = 1, 2, ..n α . We call the special kind of mixture of Eq. 75 a type-e statistical mixture, to emphasize that now the electrons are in a intrinsically mixed state.

In this situation the fact that a unique nuclear wavefunction can be singled out from the ensemble means that the electronic states |u α (x) 's have only an overall gauge degree of freedom, i.e. their phases cannot be changed independently of each other. Rather, a single set of gauge potentials exist, A k for k = 0, 1, 2, .. (in keeping with the previous notation we use k = 0 for the time coordinate) and this defines completely the dynamical problem for the nuclei and makes the theory yet relatively simple. We notice that this does not imply that the |u α 's define the same Berry connection, only that the gauge potentials are just the ensemble average
div.formula: A k = α w α A α k ≡ α ω α (i u α |∂ k u α )(76)
div.p: in such a way that the same gauge transformation applied to the |u α 's, i.e. |u α → e -iϕ |u α , gives rise to the desired transformation law for the gauge potentials
div.formula: A k → A k + ∂ k ϕ
div.p: In turn, this also implies that both the pseudo-magnetic and pseudo-electric fields are ensemble averages of the pure-state expressions (see Eq. 7)
div.formula: B kj = α ω α (∂ k A α j -∂ j A α k ) ≡ α ω α B α kj E k = α ω α (∂ k A α 0 -∂ t A α k ) ≡ α ω α E α k (77)
div.p: However, in order to completely define the nuclear problem, we shall also need an ensemble average for the metric tensor, g av ij . This requires separate considerations (see below, Eq. 84) since for mixed states the tensor cannot be represented in terms of derivatives of the conditional electronic density operator. Once this issue is settled, the nuclear dynamics follows straightforwardly from the pure-state case, and can be subsumed in the gauge-invariant quantum hydrodynamical equations, e.g. continuity equation for the nuclear density (Eq. 37) and the momentum equation of Eq. 57,
div.formula: dπ k dt = -Tr e (ρ el ∂ k H el )- 2 n ij ξ ij ∂ i (ng av kj )+(F q ) k (78)
div.p: with the quantum force (F q ) k , as usual, given by Eq. 38.

Thus, we are left with the problem of seeking a gauge invariant formulation of the electron dynamics that can account for the mixed state character of the local electronic states. Formally, given a total density operator ρ(x, x ′ ) and a nuclear wavefunction ψ(x) (hence, the pure-state apparent nuclear density matrix σ(x, x ′ ) = ψ(x)ψ * (x ′ )) we can define a "doubly conditional electronic operator" according to
div.formula: Ŵ(x, x ′ ) = ρ(x, x ′ ) σ(x, x ′ )
div.p: for any x and x ′ where the wavefunction does not vanish. This operator determines the conditional density operator for electrons through its diagonal elements
div.formula: ρ el (q) = Ŵ(q, q)
div.p: and, in general, determines the true nuclear density matrix through its off diagonal elements
div.formula: ρ n (x, x ′ ) = σ(x, x ′ )Tr el Ŵ(x, x ′ )
div.p: These "doubly conditional" electronic operators Ŵ(x, x ′ ) are the key elements of the theory of type-e statistical mixtures. In the following we shall describe them by means of a set of moments, which we call mechanical electronic moments, in full analogy with the mechanical momentum moments of Sections II D and III B. We report only the main findings of these developments and leave the details of the calculations in Appendix D. We start defining the "mechanical" electronic moments M(0) (q) = Ŵ(q, q) ≡ ρ el (q)
div.formula: k (q) = µ k + µ ′ * k 2 Ŵ(x, x ′ )| x=x ′ =q M(2) kj (q) = µ k + µ ′ * k 2 µ j + µ ′ * j 2 Ŵ(x, x ′ )| x=x ′ =q ... where now µ k = -i ∂ k + A k ≡ -i D k and D k = ∂ k +
div.p: iA k is the gauge covariant derivative. By definition, these moments are gauge invariant with respect to the gauge transformations mentioned above (note that sign change in the gauge potentials when compared to the mechanical momenta of the nuclei, i.e., µ k ≡ -π * k ). For a pure state, for instance, we find
div.formula: kj (q) = 2 4 (|D k u(q) D j u(q)| + |D j u(q) D k u(q)|) + - 2 4 (|D k D j u(q) u(q)| + |u(q) D k D j u(q)|)(81)
div.p: The equations of motion for these moments can be derived from an analysis of the pure-state case, provided the results are cast in a form that survives the ensemble average (see Appendix D). The appropriate form of the electronic equation for the state vectors involves only the gauge covariant derivatives D k 's, since these are fixed in our setting by the presence of a single set of gauge potentials A k . This is Eq. 54 in the form
div.formula: i |D 0 u = (H el -V) |u -i j V j |D j u + - 2 2 ij ξ ij |D i D j u(82)
div.p: where we have set D 0 ≡ ∂ t + iA 0 and removed the projector Q = 1 -P , P = |u u|, which is annoying for the ensemble average. This could be done at the price of adding the following term in the space projected by P ("P -space")
div.formula: V = E el + 2 2 ij ξ ij g ij ≡ u|H el |u + 2 2 ij ξ ij D i u|D j u
div.p: which is precisely the potential energy for the nuclear motion (noting that D i u|D j u ≡ ∂ i u|Q|∂ j u ), and which guarantees u|D 0 u ≡ 0. Indeed, we have
div.formula: i u|D 0 u = (E el -V) - 2 2 ij ξ ij u|D i D j u ≡ 0 since u|D j u = 0 implies u|D i D j u ≡ -D i u|D j u .
div.p: When extending the above equation to a statistical mixture of the type considered here, i.e. with the replacement |u → |u α for α = 1, 2, ..n α in Eq. 82, orthogonality no longer holds for each representative
div.formula: u α |D 0 u α = i(A 0 -A α 0 ) A α 0 := i u α |∂ t u α but holds on average if we set V = α ω α ( u α |H el |u α + 2 2 ij ξ ij D i u α |D j u α )
div.p: (83) since this implies
div.formula: (A α 0 -A 0 ) = j V j (A j -A α j )+ -i 2 2 ij ξ ij ∂ i (A j -A α j ) ≡ 0
div.p: Here, we have used the overbar to denote the ensemble average and exploited the definition of the ensemble connection, Eq. 76.

It is worth stressing at this point that the electronic equation of motion for the ensemble realizations (Eq. 82 with |u α in place of |u ) remains gauge covariant, even if the A k 's are not defined by any Berry connection. Indeed, for that property to hold one only needs that the potentials transform as A k → A k + ∂ k ϕ under the gauge transformation |u α → e -iϕ |u α . This is the essence of the gauge covariant derivative and it is the key for Eq. 82 to work properly for the members of our ensemble [54].
Incidentally, the above arguments also show that the metric tensor entering the nuclear potential V (Eq. 83) is g av ij ≡ ℜq av ij where
div.formula: q av ij = D i u α |D j u α(84)
div.p: is the appropriate expression for the averaged quantum geometric tensor. This is consistent with the expression for the average gauge fields given in Eq. 77 and formally settles any issue concerning the nuclear dynamics. Now, as shown in Appendix D, when using Eq. 82 as effective electronic equation for our type-e ensemble, the zero-th moment equation takes the form
div.formula: d M(0) dt (q) = - i [H el (q), M(0) (q)]+ - 1 n(q) ij ξ ij ∂ i n(q) M(1) j (q)(85)
div.p: where d/dt denotes the material derivative and n(q) = |ψ(x)| 2 is the usual nuclear density. This is actually one of the several possible ways of presenting the equation of motion for the conditional density of a pure state (see Appendix C), the one appropriate for the kind of ensemble averages we are interested here. The key difference between a type-e mixed state and a pure state is that only for the latter the first order moment appearing on the r.h.s. can be expressed in terms of the zero-th order one and its spatial derivative,
div.formula: M(1) j = i 2 [ M(0) , ∂ j M(0) ] (pure state)
div.p: Eq. 85 is a Liouville-von Neumann-like equation with a surface term (second term on the r.h.s.) that resembles the flux term of the continuity equation. Upon multiplying by n(q) and integrating over all nuclear configuration space we obtain
div.formula: ∂γ el ∂t = - i ˆdq n(q) [H el (q), M(0) (q)](86)
div.p: where γ el is the "true" density operator of the electronic subsystem, appropriate to the present statistical mixture (see Eq. 65),
div.formula: γ el = ˆdq n(q) M(0) (q)(87)
div.p: This equation generalizes Eq. 74 found for pure states, and is subjected to the same caveats: most often one needs the electronic moment M(0) (q) (and the nuclear density n(q)) for computing the observables of interest, hence the global electronic density operator γ el is of little help. Now, the main difference between the type-e statistical mixture addressed here and the pure-state case of Sec. III A is that the evolution of the zero-th electronic moment requires the first moment (last term on the r.h.s. of Eq. 85), which in turn involves higher order moments. In other words, the Liouville von Neumann like equation of Eq. 85 no longer suffices to describe the electron dynamics, and an infinite hierarchy of equations is necessary. For instance, the dynamical equation of the first order moments reads as
div.formula: d M(1)

k dt = - i [H el , M(1) k ] - 1 2 ∂ k H el -∂ k H el , M (0) + + + 2 n ij ξ ij ∂ i (ng av kj ) M(0) + - ij ξ ij (∂ i ln n)M (2) kj (88) - ij ξ ij ∂ i M(2) kj - ij ξ ij B ki M(1) j + + i 2 ij ξ ij (∂ k ∂ i ln n) M(1) j - j (∂ k v j ) M(1)
div.p: j

The evolution of these moments requires the second-order moments in a flux-like term (fourth line), even if the e-n coupling term (third line) is neglected. This is the general way the couplings to higher order moments occurs. In fact, higher order moment equations become increasingly complicated, but they appear unlikely to play a key role in practical applications.
Before closing this Section we notice that the electronic moments have well defined traces,
div.formula: Tr el M(0) = 1 Tr M(1) k = 0 Tr M(2) kj = 2 g av kj (89)
div.p: as can be checked with a direct calculation on the purestate expressions (Eqs. 79, 80 and 81), followed by an ensemble average. The zero-th moment equation, Eq. 85, is clearly trace-conserving since both the commutator and the first moment on the r.h.s. are traceless. As for the first moment equation, on the other hand, the trace of the r.h.s. of Eq. 88 reduces to
div.formula: 2 n ij ξ ij ∂ i (ng av kj ) -2 ij ξ ij (∂ i ln n)g av kj + -2 ij ξ ij ∂ i g av kj ≡ 0
div.p: thereby proving that this equation is trace-conserving too.

As a final remark we notice that the type-e statistical mixture considered in this Section requires the instantaneous pseudo-magnetic field B kj = -2 ℑq av kj , as is evident from the moment equation of first order given in Eq. 88. This cannot be obtained from Eq. 73 since the latter only holds for pure states, and cannot be generalized to statistical mixtures. The situation differs from that of the metric g av kj , which is directly provided by the second moments through their trace (Eq. 89). The required equation of motion for B kj can be obtained from Maxwell-Faraday induction law (Eq. 8) and is conveniently re-expressed in terms of the forces acting on the nuclei since these are in any case needed for the particle dynamics. It reads as
div.formula: - ∂B kj ∂t = ∂ k F j -∂ j F k + m ∂ j (v m B km ) -∂ k (v m B jm )(90
div.p: ) where F k can either be the classical force
div.formula: F k = -Tr e (ρ el ∂ k H el ) - 2 n ij ξ ij ∂ i (ng av kj )
div.p: or the total force of Eq. 78 (the quantum component is longitudinal and does not contribute to the curl appearing in Eq. 90) . Eq. 90 follows from Eq. 58 upon singling out the electric field components and performing the ensemble average. It plays the role of a vortex equation for the dynamics of the quantum probability fluid.

div.head: D. Remark on mechanical momentum moments
div.p: The momentum moments introduced in the previous Sections replace in a sense the ordinary moments and, as seen above, provide a gauge invariant description of the dynamics. However, they are not (separately) fully equivalent to the ordinary moments: the latter allow, at least in principle, to retrieve a density matrix, while this is not obviously the case for the mechanical moments which, by definition, require the gauge potentials.
The point, however, is that, after all, we are always interested in the full density matrix
div.formula: ρ(x, x ′ ) = σ(x, x ′ ) Ŵ(x, x ′ )
div.p: The issue of the gauge invariance only arises when looking separately at the electronic and the nuclear problems, the total density matrix above is in fact globally gauge invariant by construction. The mechanical moments allow one to split the problem into gauge invariant sub-problems. This becomes clear when focusing on the ordinary moments of this matrix, for instance
Here,
div.formula: -ih∂ k σ(x, x ′ ) Ŵ(x, x ′ ) = (π k σ(x, x ′ )) Ŵ(x, x ′ )+ + σ(x, x ′ ) µ k Ŵ(x, x ′ )
div.p: where
div.formula: π k = -i ∂ k -A k and µ k = -i ∂ k + A k as defined above, hence Q(1) k (q) = M(1)
div.p: k (q) M(0) (q) + M (0) (q) M( 1) k (q) This equation shows that, even though the nuclear (electronic) mechanical moments are not sufficient to obtain the nuclear (electronic) density matrix, the set of mechanical moments for both electrons and nuclei does provide a complete description of the system, i.e., the full density operator.

The "weakness" of the mechanical moments is only apparent, it is actually a necessity: the marginal density matrix for the nuclei and the conditional density matrix for the electrons -as inspired by the exact factorization of the total wavefunction -are gauge dependent, hence require that a gauge choice is made.

div.head: IV. APPLICATIONS A. Mixed quantum-classical dynamics
div.p: In this Section we make a connection between the exact electron-nuclear quantum dynamics formulated for a pure state in Section III A and commonly adopted mixed quantum-classical schemes in which nuclei are treated as classical particles and electrons evolve quantum dynamically. The purpose of this Section is to bridge the exact quantum theory to practical approaches to the problem and, eventually, to identify the key corrections to the latter that can bring results in better agreement with the exact one.

We start discussing a little point that concerns the dynamical interpretation behind Eq. 55 and the original equation arising from the exact factorization of the electron-nuclear wavefunction, namely Eq. 4 or the equivalent
div.formula: i Q∂ t |u(x, t) = QH el (x) |u(x, t) -i j v j Q∂ j |u(x, t) + j u j Q∂ j |u(x, t) -R |u(x, t)(91)
div.p: Eq. 91 is formulated in the Eulerian frame: for each x in the configuration space of the system there exists an equation of motion of the kind of Eq. 91, and the local electronic state fixed at x is seen to evolve under the action of one and the same electronic Hamiltonian H el (x) at any time, besides its coupling to the nuclear motion [55]. Eq. 55, on the other hand, is formulated in the Lagrangian frame, as appropriate for instance for a "direct" dynamical approach: for each evolving representative x t there exists an electronic equation of the kind of Eq. 55 and the electronic state evolves under the action of the instantaneous Hamiltonian H el (x t ) along the particle trajectory describing nuclear motion in the QHD setting,
div.formula: i Q d dt |u(x t ) = QH el (x t ) |u(x t ) + + j u j Q∂ j |u(x t ) -R |u(x t )(92)
div.p: Albeit equivalent in an exact framework, the two equations, Eqs. 91 and 92, represent distinct starting points when introducing approximations. In the Lagrangian picture the coupling to the nuclear motion appears reduced, since the effect of the (real) velocity fields has been absorbed in the total time derivative, with clear advantages in the diabatic (fast moving nuclei) limit where the electronic state has hardly time to adjust to the "evolving" Hamiltonian. On the other hand, the advection term becomes a complicating factor in the adiabatic limit where the electrons respond rapidly to the changing Hamiltonian, on the time scale appropriate for a (slow) nuclear motion. This latter, adiabatic limit deserves some considerations since it is rather instructive. It is easily formulated in the Eulerian frame, where the adiabatic condition
is a stationary condition for the local electronic states that, we remind, are fixed in space in this picture. In the Lagrangian frame, on the other hand, it reads as
div.formula: i Q d dt |u(x t ) ≈ i j v j Q∂ j |u (94
div.p: and it is a bit less intuitive. It becomes clear when the adiabatic dynamics is driven by classical parameters x k , with velocity ẋk ≡ v k , since in that case the appropriate (variational) equation of motion would be (see Eq. 4 in Ref. [28])
div.formula: i d dt |u = EP |u + i Ṗ |u (95
div.p: where P is the eigenprojector of interest, E the corresponding eigenvalue and Ṗ its directional derivative Ṗ = j v j ∂ j P . Indeed, Eq. 94 follows from Eq. 95 upon projecting on Q space
div.formula: Q d dt |u = j v j Q∂ j (|u u|) |u ≡ j v j Q∂ j |u
div.p: and noticing that the dynamics in the P -space is just a gauge choice. Clearly, if the dynamics is close to be adiabatic the Euler form is preferred and the stationary condition is conveniently turned into a standard eigenvalue problem -i.e., QH el |u = 0 -in the limit where v k → 0 and the e-n coupling terms on the r.h.s of Eq. 91 can be neglected. Far from this dynamical limit, on the other hand, the Lagrangian form has distinct advantages and represents the preferred choice for describing the electron dynamics. As a matter of fact, the gauge invariant form of the electronic equation appears natural in the Lagrangian frame (Eq 66), in the Eulerian frame it would contain an "odd" advection term.
Next, we introduce a classical approximation for the nuclei by naively setting ∂ i n ≈ 0 in the equations of motions. This turns the momentum equation (Eq. 57) into
div.formula: dπ k dt ≈ -u|∂ k H el |u -2 ij ξ ij ∂ i g kj(96)
div.p: and amounts to neglect the quantum force of Eq. 38 and to simplify the dynamically corrected electric force, Eq. 63, by neglecting a term
div.formula: - 2 2 ij ξ ij g kj ∂ i ln n
div.p: at the expense of breaking a global property of the corrected electric force, i.e. the vanishing of its average over the nuclear quantum state. Eq. 96 immediately leads to classical Born-Oppenheimer dynamics if the geometric term is neglected, since u|∂ k H el |u = ∂ k u|H el |u when |u is a (normalized) eigenstate of H el . However, in order to recover the full adiabatic limit once needs to eliminate the electronic response from the above expression and enforce the adiabatic condition with the vanishing of the dynamical response F ED k (third term in Eq. 58) before setting |u to be an eigenstate of H el . This gives the well known result
div.formula: dπ k dt ≈ F k = -∂ k V + j v j B kj(97)
div.p: where V = u|H el |u + φ FS is the Born-Oppenheimer potential energy surface with the diagonal correction, and the second term is the Berry force. This can be directly checked with a calculation that starts from Eq. 96, upon noticing that
div.formula: u|∂ k H el |u = ∂ k u|H el |u -2ℜ ∂ k u|QH el |u and QH el |u ≈ i j v j Q∂ j |u + 2 2 ij ξ ij QD i D j |u
