div.p: hold in the classical, adiabatic limit, along with the geometric identity [28] ℜ
div.formula: ∂ k u|QD i D j u = 1 2 (∂ j g ik + ∂ i g kj -∂ k g ij )(98)
div.p: These considerations will turn out to be useful in the next Section where we address a quasi-adiabatic regime. Beyond this adiabatic limit, the electron dynamics needs to be explicitly taken into account and, in the classical approximation considered here, it follows the equation
div.formula: i Q d dt |u(x t ) ≈ QH el (x t ) |u(x t ) -R |u(x t )(99)
div.p: here given in the traditional wavefunction picture. This equation results from the condition ∂ i n ≈ 0 and the second term on its r.h.s. is consistent with the geometric contribution appearing in Eq. 96, meaning that the second term on the r.h.s. of Eq. 96 is precisely the dynamically corrected electric force of Eq. 63 at this level of approximation,
div.formula: F el ≈ -∂ k φ F S -2 ℜ ∂ k u|R|u ≡ -2 ij ξ ij ∂ i g kj
div.p: where we have exploited once again the geometric identity of Eq. 98. These geometrical contributions have nothing to do with the quantum nature of the nuclear dynamics and thus survive the classical limit. These are neglected in the popular Ehrenfest method, whose equations of motion
div.formula: dπ k dt ≈ -u|∂ k H el |u (100) i Q d dt |u(x t ) ≈ QH el (x t ) |u(x t )(101)
div.p: result from Eqs. 96 and 99, upon disregarding the geometric terms. Eqs. 100 and 101 are precisely the equations of motion of the "semiclassical Ehrenfest method", here obtained from first-principles without any meanfield approximation and ad-hoc correction for energy conservation. The widely used form of the Ehrenfest equations of motion is indeed easily recovered from Eqs. 100 and 101 by fixing the gauge of the electronic equation according to A 0 = E el and presenting the equations in an adiabatic (time-independent) basis of eigenstates of the electronic Hamiltonian, {|u n }. They read as
div.formula: πk = - n ρ nn ∂ k E n + n =m n,m ρ nm (E m -E n )d mn (102) ρnn = -2v k ℜ((d nk ρ kn ) (103) ρnm = - i (E n -E m )ρ nm + -v k (d nk ρ km + ρ nk (d) * mk ) (n = m)(104)
div.p: where the d nm 's are non-adiabatic coupling vectors,

are, respectively, the gradient and nuclear velocity vectors in the nuclear configuration space, and the ρ nm 's are defined by ρ nm = u n |ρ el |u m and are known as (adiabatic) populations for n = m and coherences for n = m (cf.. Eqs. 73, 74 and 78 in Ref. [56]). To arrive at Eqs. 102, 103 and 104 we expressed the off-diagonal matrix elements of the microscopic force in terms of the derivative couplings through
div.formula: u n |∂ k H el |u m = (E m -E n ) u n |∂ k u m (n = m)
div.p: and we exploited the identity
div.formula: u n | ρ|u m = ρnm -un |ρ|u m -u n |ρ| um ≡ ρnm + v k (d nk ρ km -ρ nk d km ) (105)
div.p: which follows from | un ≡ v∇ |u n and d nk = -d * kn . Clearly, a first correction to the Ehrenfest dynamics would be the inclusion of the geometric effects in both the nuclear and electron dynamics, what we can call "geometric Ehrenfest" approximation. Note that we refer here to effects that are of pseudo-electric (rather than pseudo-magnetic) nature: Berrry-like forces are absent when the electron dynamics is taken into account.

However, it turns out that a main weakness of the quantum-classical approach is not in the classical approximation to the nuclear dynamics but rather in the classical way the electron-nuclear coupling is handled. By this we mean that one can perform a classical limit for the nuclear dynamics by setting to zero the quantum force while keeping the density dependence (which occurs through the imaginary part of the nuclear momentum fields, w k = -/2∂ k ln n) in the rest. At this stage, if we neglect the geometric contributions (∂ k g ij ≈ 0) we obtain a pseudo-classical dynamics governed by
div.formula: dπ k dt ≈ -u|∂ k H el |u + 2 j u j g kj (106) i Q d dt |u(x t ) ≈ QH el (x t ) |u(x t ) + j u j |D j u (107)
div.p: where u j = i ξ ij w j is the imaginary part of the j component of the velocity field, and the rightmost terms of the two equations are again consistent with each other,
div.formula: F el ≈ δF el k ≈ 2 j u j g kj
div.p: This is pseudo-classical because the nuclear trajectories are not independent of each other, and essentially amounts to what is known in the literature as coupledtrajectory mixed quantum-classical dynamics [57][58][59], although in this context could be better named "Ehrenfest with quantum coupling". The method appears to correctly describe the wavepacket splitting arising from the non-adiabatic couplings and to fix the "overcoherence" problem which plagues traditional mixed quantumclassical approaches [56,[60][61][62]. This is achieved at the expense of introducing a coupling between trajectories through the density dependent terms u j 's that, in practice, are modeled with a Gaussian smearing of the representative points [58]. Clearly, a further improvement of the method consists in re-introducing the geometric effects, hence neglecting only the quantum force from the exact dynamical equations ("geometric Ehrenfest with quantum coupling). All these approximations are best compared when the electron dynamics is described in gauge-invariant form, i.e. by means of the Liouville-von Neumann-like equation of Eq. 66. The approximate electronic-nuclear coupling vanishes in the Ehrenfest method of Eqs. 100 and 100, while it reads as
div.formula: δH en ≈ - 2 2 ij ξ ij ∂ i ∂ j ρ el
div.p: in the geometric limit described by Eq. 99, and as δH en ≈ j u j ∂ j ρ el in the pseudo-classical limit addressed by the coupledtrajectory mixed quantum-classical method of Eqs. 106, 107. Finally, in the "geometric Ehrenfest with quantum coupling" scheme the term takes its exact form, Eq. 67,
div.formula: δH en = - 2 2n ij ξ ij ∂ i (n∂ j ρ el )
div.p: and the only approximation is the neglect of the quantum force in the nuclear equation of motion. Table II summarizes the situation and shows the key equations of motion for these mixed quantum-classical approximations that directly follow from the exact factorization of the wavefunction.

We have intentionally left trajectory surface hopping (TSH) [60] out of the discussion, since this latter method would require a separate analysis that goes well beyond the purposes of the present manuscript. TSH is a popular quantum-classical method in which electrons evolve according to the same electronic equation of the Ehrenfest method (Eq. 101) but, differently from the Ehrenfest method, the nuclei sample stochastically the energy states, according to their probabilities p n = u n |ρ el |u n . In practice, this is realized by letting the nuclei move along an energy surface (the "active" state) and hop to different states with a rate that is usually determined by the electronic equation. Since (see Eq. 105)
div.formula: -ṗn = -u n | ρel |u n + 2 j m ℜ( u n |∂ j u m ρ mn )v j
div.p: and the first term vanishes in Ehrenfest dynamics,
div.formula: u n | ρel |u n = - i u n |[H el , ρ el ]|u n ≡ 0,
div.p: we have that
div.formula: γ hop n→m = 2 p n j ℜ( u n |∂ j u m ρ mn )v j
div.p: (if positive) represents the correct transition probability from n to m, depending on the nuclear velocity, on the derivative couplings and on the coherences ρ nm .

Because of its stochastic nature, deriving TSH from deterministic equations requires additional assumptions, a topic that warrants more extensive exploration in a separate publication. Here we just notice that TSH has been almost derived [63] from the mixed quantum-classical Liouville dynamics (QCLD) developed by Martens, Kapral and others [64][65][66][67][68], and that the latter is closely related to the theory developed here. This connection becomes apparent when examining the momentum moment formulation of Eq. 69 and 70 which -similarly to QCLD -was derived from a partial Wigner transform, albeit without approximations.

In closing this Section we notice that one main problem of mixed quantum-classical methods relying on Ehrenfest evolution of the electron dynamics is the absence of any feedback from the actual distribution of the nuclei in configuration space. In the Lagrangian frame where the nuclei are at rest the electrons experience only a strictly local, potential-like coupling with the nuclei, through the electronic Hamiltonian H el (x). In the exact dynamical equation, on the other hand, the electron-nuclear coupling δH el of Eq. 67, provides a coupling which depends on (the gradient of) n(x) and which is active when ρ el happens to change in the neighborhoods of x (since it depends on ∂ j ρ el ). In terms of hopping probabilities, adding this e-n coupling contribution introduces a correction to the transition probability. Indeed, differently from Ehrenfest dynamics, we now have
div.formula: u n | ρel |u n = 2 ℑ u n |δH en ρ el |u n
div.p: hence the hopping probabilities get modified into
div.formula: γ hop n→m → γhop n→m = γ hop n→m - 2 p n ℑ (X nm ρ mn )
div.p: Electron dynamics ( ρel ) Classical forces ( πk )
div.formula: EhrenfestI (i ) -1 [H el , ρ el ] -Tr el (ρ el (∂ k H el )) geometric EhrenfestI (i ) -1 [H el - 2 2 ∆M ρ el , ρ el ] -Tr el (ρ el (∂ k H el )) -2 ij ξ ij ∂ig kj Ehrenfest with quantum couplingI (i ) -1 [H el + j u j ∂jρ el , ρ el ] -Tr el (ρ el (∂ k H el )) + 2 j u j g kj geometric Ehrenfest with quantum couplingI (i ) -1 [H el - 2 2n ij ξ ij ∂i(n∂jρ el ), ρ el ] -Tr el (ρ el (∂ k H el )) - 2 n ij ξ ij ∂i(ng kj )
div.p: Table II. Summary of the mixed quantum-classical methods that originate from the exact factorization of the wavefunction, as described in the main text. The second column defines the electron dynamics in gauge invariant form by providing the expression for the Lagrangian rate of change of the conditional density operator ρ el . The third column defines correspondingly the nuclear dynamics, by providing the k th component of the force acting on the (classically) moving nuclei, in terms of guage invariant electronic quantities. In the equations ∆M is the mass-weighted Laplacian of Eq. 40, u j is the imaginary part of the velocity field, u j = -2 i ξ ji ∂i ln n (Sec. II A), n is the nuclear density and g kj is the Fubini-Study metric tensor,
div.formula: g kj = ℜTr el (ρ el (∂ k ρ el )(∂jρ el )).
div.p: where
div.formula: X nm = 1 u n |δH en |u m = -2 u n |∆ M ρ el |u m + j u j u n |∂ j ρ el |u m
div.p: Here, the first term is geometric, while the second is dynamical and depends on the nuclear density gradient through the imaginary component of the velocity field, u j . Neglecting the first term one obtains a corrected transition probability in the form
div.formula: γhop n→m ≈ - 2 p n j ℜ((∂ j P n ) nm ρ mn )v j + - 2 p n j ℑ ((∂ j ρ el ) nm ρ mn ) u j
div.p: where u n |∂ j u m =u n |∂ j P n |u m has been used for m = n. This correction turns TSH into a coupled trajectory surface hopping method (where coupling occurs through the hopping probabilities only) and might be important when ρ el deviates from a pure adiabatic state (otherwise u n | ρel |u n ≡ 0, as in the ordinary TSH, and the correction vanishes). Of course, if the electron-nuclear term δH en were also considered for the electron dynamics (even at an approximate level) it would provide a further source of coupling for the trajectories and it would directly affect the evolution of populations and coherences. The specific form of electronic decoherence introduced by this electronnuclear quantum coupling will be addressed in a separate publication. Here, we just notice that, as observed in Sec. III A, the exact electronic equation, Eq. 74, is purity conserving, as it must be since it is just the electronic equation for the pure-state, local electronic wavefunction defined by the exact factorization approach (i.e., Eq. 4), recast in a density form. Hence, if decoherence happens to occur in the energy basis between regions of strong non-adiabatic coupling it must be accompanied by population decay to a single adiabatic state, i.e., it must be of the "decay of mixing" type, according to the classification of Ref. [56].

div.head: B. Electronic friction at finite temperature
div.p: Quoting Dou et al. [69], "electronic friction is a correction to the Born-Oppenheimer approximation, whereby nuclei in motion experience a drag in the presence of a manifold of electronic states". It is one of the many non-adiabatic phenomena that may occur when atoms or molecules scatter, vibrate or react at or close to a metal surface [9,13,69,70]. It is the simplest one, since it is the first correction to the adiabatic picture: during their motion, nuclei induce e-h pair excitations into the metal thereby transferring energy to the substrate, but the excitation is of such limited extent that it can be quickly "moved away", on the time-scale of the nuclear motion. As a result, the electronic state is always found close to a local adiabatic state (the ground-state for T = 0 K), and the nuclear dynamics is quasi-adiabatic with an added friction.

The T = 0 K electronic frictional regime was derived by the present authors [27,28], under suitable assumptions, from the exact factorization of the electron-nuclear wavefunction. The derivation was relatively simple -an application of linear response theory to the electronic motion described by Eq. 54-and it should be now clear why: we adopted the Eulerian frame which, as discussed in the previous Section, is the most appropriate frame for handling a quasi-adiabatic dynamics. In fact, the derivation of a memory kernel involving the past evolution of the local electronic states would be a formidable task in the Lagrangian frame, where the past behavior of the electronic state is determined by the electronic Hamiltonian H el (x τ ) evaluated along the trajectory that brings the nuclear coordinates to x t at time t.

This issue actually poses a fundamental interpretative problem when extending classical approaches to memory (as opposed to memoryless or Markov) friction: how can the system found at x at time t experience a memory, in the electronic response, that depends only on H el (x), if it never visited x at earlier times? The memory, if any, should involve the past dynamics, hence H el (x) along the visited trajectory. The reason why we do get a coordinate-local memory (in spite of the oddities it implies for the nuclear dynamics in the classical limit) is, we believe, a manifestation of non-locality of quantum mechanics, here arising in an apparently "innocent" setting: depending on the time-scale of the electron dynamics, the representative point found at x at time t may experience the effect of a perturbation in the electronic state |u(x, t) which was induced by different representatives that visited x in the past.

Having elaborated on these interpretative issues -and made clear that a re-derivation of the electronic friction picture in the Lagrangian frame is a challenging task when memory effects are non-negligible -let us first recast the results of our previous work [27,28] in the hydrodynamical setting developed in this work. These results can be summarized as follows. At T = 0 K, provided the ground-state is non-degenerate, the whole en state is pure and the quantum nuclear dynamics is described by Eq. 57 with the total classical force F k provided by Eq. 64, evaluated with the evolving electronic state |u . The latter is assumed to be always close to the ground-state |u 0 , hence
div.formula: F k = F 0 k + δF k where F 0
div.p: k is the ground-state, dynamically corrected force of Eq. 64 and δF k = 2ℜ ∂ k u 0 |QH el |∆u to linear order in |∆u = |u -|u 0 (upon neglecting terms that depend on the spatial derivatives of |∆u and that arise from the geometric contribution g ij ). The electronic fluctuation |∆u can be obtained from linear response theory using Eq. 54 and it leads to
div.formula: δF k = -2 j ℜ ˆ∞ 0 Γ kj (τ )V j (x, t -τ ) dτ + F geom k
div.p: where the first term is a pseudo-friction contribution with the kernel
div.formula: Γ kj (t) = ∂ k u 0 |Q 0 H ′ el e -i H ′ el t |∂ j u 0 (108)
div.p: and the second is a geometric contribution
div.formula: F geom k = 2 ℜ ∂ k u 0 |Ru 0 + -2π ∂ k u 0 |Q 0 H ′ el δ(H ′ el )|Ru 0 (109)
div.p: Here, H ′ el is the ground-state referenced, local Hamiltonian, i.e., H ′ el = H el (x) -E 0 (x), and Q 0 = |u 0 u 0 |. In the memoryless (Markov) limit only the integrated kernel matters and this can be recast as
div.formula: γkj = 2 lim ǫ→0 ˆ∞ 0 e -ǫt Γ kj (t) dt = -2i q 0 kj + γ kj (110)
div.p: where q 0 kj is the ground-state geometric tensor and
div.formula: γ kj = π lim ω→0 + u 0 |(∂ k H el )Q 0 δ( ω -H ′ el )Q 0 (∂ j H el )|u 0 ω
div.p: is the Markovian friction kernel. Hence, upon neglecting the second term in Eq. 109 (which is non-zero only when time-reversal invariance is broken), the correction force in the Markov limit reads as
div.formula: δF k ≈ F friction k + F mag k -δF el k where F friction k = -ℜ   j γ kj V j   (111
div.p: is the electronic friction contribution, F mag k = j v j B kj and δF el k is the dynamical correction to the pseudoelectric force defined in Eq. 61. In other words, the Markov-limit quantum hydrodynamical equation of motion governing the nuclear dynamics is
div.formula: dπ k dt = F ad k + F friction k + (F q ) k (112)
div.p: where F ad k is the ground-state adiabatic force of Eq. 97 and (F q ) k is the quantum force of Eq. 38.

Next, we consider electronic friction at finite temperature, using the theory developed in Section III C for typee statistical mixtures. This is the useful framework in the mixed quantum-classical limit where the electronic friction regime is most often considered, henceforth we shall start with re-deriving the results recently obtained by Duo, Miao & Subotnik (DMS) [71] in a mixed quantumclassical framework. To this end we neglect the "quantum couplings" and the geometric terms in the nuclear equation of motion (Eq. 78) and truncate the momentum moment hierarchy for the electrons at the zero-th order (see Eq. 85), i.e., we consider
div.formula: i ρel ≈ [H el , ρ el ] dπ k dt ≈ -Tr el (ρ el ∂ k H el )
div.p: where we have set ρ el ≡ M(0) for clarity. This is clearly the mixed-state generalization of the simple Ehrenfest method discussed in the previous Section. Next, we apply linear response theory to the electron dynamics in the Eulerian frame, treating the advection term as a perturbation. The result is
div.formula: ρ el (t) = ρ 0 el - j ˆ∞ 0 v j (t -τ )e -i H el τ ∂ j ρ 0 el e + i H el τ dτ
div.p: (113) where ρ 0 el is the density operator that characterizes the electronic system at steady-state. Eq. 113 can then be used to evaluate the force governing the nuclear dynamics
div.formula: F k = -Tr el (ρ el (∂ k H el )) ≈ F 0 k - j ˆ∞ 0 W kj (τ )v j (t -τ )dτ
div.p: where
div.formula: F 0 k = -Tr el ρ 0 el (∂ k H el )
div.p: is the average microscopic force acting on the nuclei at steady state and the kernel reads as
div.formula: W kj (τ ) = -Tr el e + i H el τ (∂ k H el ) e -i H el τ ∂ j ρ 0 el = Tr el f k (τ ) ∂ j ρ 0 el = Tr el δf k (τ ) ∂ j ρ 0 el (114)
div.p: Here, we have introduced the (Heisenberg evolved) microscopic force operator
div.formula: f k (τ ) = -e + i H el τ (∂ k H el ) e -i H el τ
div.p: and its fluctuation
div.formula: δf k (t) = f k (t) -F 0 k (115)
div.p: upon exploiting Tr el (∂ j ρ 0 el ) ≡ 0. In the Markov limit only the integrated kernel matters and we have
div.formula: F k ≈ F 0 k - j γ DMS kj v j with γ DMS kj = lim ǫ→0 + ˆ∞ 0 e -ǫt W kj (t) dt
div.p: These are the results obtained by DMS in their mixed quantum-classical theory of electronic friction [71].

In our previous work [27,28] we showed that in the T = 0 K limit the DMS kernel reduces to the real part of Eq. 110 -i.e., γ DMS kj → ℜγ kj at vanishing temperature -and that γkj is actually a pseudo-friction kernel since it also contains a pseudo-magnetic contribution (see the quantum geometric contribution in the rightmost hand side of Eq. 110). The same applies at finite temperatures, where W kj and its integrated version γ DMS kj are real by construction but not necessarily symmetric in their indexes, unless time-reversal symmetry holds. Hence, in general, γ DMS kj = η kj -B DMS kj with η kj symmetric and B DMS kj antisymmetric, and we have, in the Markov limit,
div.formula: F k ≈ F 0 k - j η kj v j + j B DMS kj v j (116)
div.p: Here, only the second term represents a true frictional force, the last one is a pseudo-magnetic force. The above results hold for a general steady-state. If the electronic system is in (local) canonical equilibrium additional, interesting properties hold. First of all, the force F 0 k becomes an equilibrium force which is manifestly conservative,
div.formula: F 0 k = n (-∂ k E n )e -βEn n e -βEn ≡ 1 β ∂ k ln Z el = -∂ k Φ el
div.p: where n labels the eigenstates of H el , Z el = n e -βEn is the (local) partition function of the electronic system and Φ el = -k B T ln Z β -the corresponding (coordinatedependent) Helmholtz free-energy -is the appropriate "thermal" potential that generates the force. Secondly, the kernel W kj can be recast as a Kubo correlation function
div.formula: W kj (t) = βTr el ρ 0 el (δf j ) β K δf k (t) (117)
div.p: where, in general,
div.formula: A β K = 1 β ˆβ 0 e +αH Ae -αH dα
div.p: is the Kubo transform of the operator A at the inverse temperature β (H ≡ H el in our problem). This follows directly from the definition of the kernel upon noticing the identity (see Appendix E)
div.formula: ∂ j ρ 0 el = βρ 0 el (δf j ) β K
div.p: Eq. 117 is a form of the fluctuation-dissipation theorem of the second kind relating the (time-symmetrized) memory kernel and the correlation function of the "random force" δf k , and gives the Markovian kernel in Green-Kubo form
div.formula: γ DMS kj = β lim ǫ→ 0 + ˆ∞ 0 e -ǫt K β δf k ,δfj(t)dt
div.p: where
div.formula: K β AB (t) = Tr ρB β K A(t)
div.p: for A = δf k and B = δf j . All this connects electronic friction theory with linear-response irreversible thermodynamics, a rather interesting issue that deserves consideration in a separate manuscript. Here, we just notice that in thermal equilibrium the pseudo-magnetic field appearing in the Markov-limit expression of Eq. 116 is given by B DMS kj = -2 ℑq av kj , where q av kj is the quantum geometric tensor defined in Eq. 84, averaged over the canonical ensemble (see Appendix F). Likewise, the "corrected" Markovian friction kernel reads, in thermal equilibrium, as
div.formula: η kj = lim ω→0 ℜγ β kj (ω)(1 -e -β ω )(118)
div.p: where
div.formula: γ β kj (ω) = π ω n p n u n |(∂ k H el )Q n δ( ω -H n el )(∂ j H el )|u n (119) satisfies the detailed balance condition γ β kj (ω) = -e β ω γ β jk (-ω)(120)
div.p: as shown in detail in Appendix F. Here, the p n 's are Boltzmann populations, n labels again energy eigenstates,
div.formula: Q n = 1 -|u n u n | and H n el = H el -E n .
div.p: Clearly, the difference with the T = 0 K case is due to the electronic de-excitation processes that can occur in the electronic bath at finite temperature and that work by pumping energy into the nuclear system. Detailed balance guarantees that the kernel ℜγ β kj (ω) remains positive definite for ω > 0, hence it correctly describes friction. Finally, Appendix G shows that for independent electrons Eq. 118 becomes
div.formula: η kj = lim ω→0 π ω ℜ ab D k ab D j ba (f (ǫ a ) -f (ǫ b ))δ( ω -∆ǫ ba )(121)
div.p: where D k ab = φ a |∂ k h|φ b , |φ a (ǫ a ) are single-particle states (energies), h is the single-particle Hamiltonian, ∆ǫ ba = ǫ bǫ a and f (ǫ) is the Fermi occupation function at the given temperature. Eq. 121 is the finitetemperature extension of the original Head-Gordon and Tully expression [72],
div.formula: η kj = π ab D k ab D j ba δ(ǫ a -ǫ F )δ(ǫ b -ǫ F )
div.p: to which it reduces in the limit T → 0 K (see Appendix G). So far we have considered electronic friction theory in the context of the simple Ehrenfest theory for mixedstates. Improvements are possible (in a yet mixed quantum-classical framework) along the lines of arguments used in Section IV B, by either introducing geometric contributions or describing the e-n couplings quantally or both. The main difference with Section IV B is that now the hierarchy of electronic moment equations does not terminate at the zero-th order (that of the conditional density operators), rather requires the first moment equation to introduce corrections (Eq. 88). The latter can be simplified to its free-evolution
div.formula: d M(1)
k dt ≈ - i [H el , M(1) k ]
div.p: and handled in linear response theory, consistently with zero-th moment equation, giving rise to correction terms that account for the quantum couplings. We discuss these improvements in a forthcoming paper.

div.head: V. SUMMARY AND CONCLUSIONS
div.p: This study has delved into the quantum dynamics of electron-nuclear systems by leveraging the concept of exact factorization of the wavefunction. Our primary objective was to reformulate the equations of motion for both electrons and nuclei in a manner that eliminates the dependence on the arbitrary phase of the electronic (and nuclear) wavefunction, leaving only physical contributions.

In the case of pure states, we achieved this in an exact way by adopting a quantum hydrodynamical description for the nuclei, coupled with the dynamics of specific density operators for electrons. These density operators are conditional, meaning that they depend on the nuclei being in a particular configuration, and therefore move in tandem with the fluid elements that describe the nuclear dynamics. This is a setup often invoked in modern quantum approaches to the problem where the dynamics is "direct" and the electronic problem is solved "on-the-fly" alongside the propagation of the nuclear wavefunction. The advantage of the proposed approach is that it removes completely the need of selecting smooth phases for the wavefunctions. Importantly, the proposed approach establishes a natural connection between exact quantum dynamics and various mixed quantum-classical methodologies, laying the groundwork for their systematic enhancement. This is where direct dynamical approaches are mostly employed and where a protocol for systematic corrections is yet missing.

The present theory further enables the extension of the exact factorization approach to encompass statistical mixtures of states, where either the nuclei or the electrons are in a inherently mixed state. The primary adjustment in this scenario involves a hierarchical construction that incorporates moments associated with the variables (either electrons or nuclei) existing in a mixed state. These advancements establish a formal connection to approximate non-adiabatic theories in condensed phases, where nuclei are often classical and electrons are in a mixed state. As an illustrative example, we explored the concept of electronic friction at finite temperatures, in the quantum-classical limit where it is commonly invoked.

In concluding, we emphasize the role played by the exact factorization of the wavefunction in the developments presented in this work. It emerges as a key tool for investigating electron-nuclear systems, forming the basis for unifying many different themes and views in molecular dynamics.

div.head: Appendix A: Circulation theorem
div.p: The quantization condition on the circulation of the (Hamiltonian) momentum field given in Eq. 12, i.e.,
div.formula: Γ γ = k ˛γ dx k p k = 2π n n ∈ Z with p k ≡ π k + A k ,
div.p: is necessary to single out from the many solutions of the hydrodynamical equations of motion (Eqs. 34 and 37) those that are compatible with the Schrödinger equation we started from. It guarantees the existence of a smooth scalar function S(x), that can be used to synthesize the wavefunction ψ(x) = n(x) exp(iS(x)/ ), once the solutions {n(x), π k (x)} of the hydrodynamical problem have been obtained. This is the open-path line integral along the curve γ(x 0 , x) joining x to a reference point x 0
div.formula: S(x) = ˆγ(x0,x) dx k (π k + A k )
div.p: that is in fact path-independent (modulo 2π ) under the quantization condition of Eq. 12. However, for consistency with the dynamical evolution one has to check that this condition is preserved during the dynamics, i.e. dΓ γ /dt = 0. We first consider the simple case where the loop γ is fixed in space and
div.formula: d dt Γ γ = k ˛γ dx k ∂π k ∂t + ∂A k ∂t
div.p: This is easily seen to vanish since, upon taking the real part of Eq. 27, i.e.,
div.formula: ∂π k ∂t + ∂A k ∂t = -∂ k ℜ Hψ ψ
div.p: one sees that the integrand is in fact purely longitudinal and therefore gives a vanishing contribution to the closedpath line integral. The only (implicit) assumption here is that in the flow of the probability fluid no wavefunction node crosses the loop γ, otherwise p k would change abruptly and ∂p k /∂t would be singular at the crossing point (see Section II A). If this condition is satisfied we thus have
div.formula: k ˛γ dx k p k (t) = k ˛γ dx k p k (t ′ )
div.p: for arbitrary times t and t ′ . Let then be γ ′ the loop γ that has been transported by the flow v k from t to t ′ . We also have
div.formula: k ˛γ dx k p k (t ′ ) = k ˛γ′ dx k p k (t ′ )
div.p: since Eq. 12 holds for arbitrary loops and γ and γ ′ are connected by a continuous transformation that cannot change the value of n. Hence, Γ γ is preserved along closed paths that are in motion with the fluid elements. This is Kelvin's circulation theorem for the many-body quantum hydrodynamical fluid investigated in Sec. II C, i.e., that describing the nuclear dynamics in the exact factorization approach of Sec. III. The final result can also be obtained with a direct calculation that bypasses the need of a non-crossing condition. Indeed, when the curve follows the fluid, the rate of change of circulation involves the material derivative of the fields and contains contributions from the moving path
div.formula: d dt k ˛γ dx k (π k + A k ) = k ˛γ dx k ( πk + Ȧk )+ + kj ˛γ dx k (π j + A j )∂ k v j
div.p: Here, the second term on the r.h.s. can be rearranged as
div.formula: kj ˛γ dx k (π j + A j )∂ k v j = = ˛γ kj dx k ∂ k [(π j + A j )v j ] - ˛γ kj dx k v j ∂ k [(π j + A j )] = - ˛γ kj dx k v j ∂ k π j - ˛γ kj dx k v j B kj + - ˛γ kj dx k v j ∂ j A k
div.p: upon noticing that the first integral on the second line vanishes identically. In second step we have introduced the magnetic field (Eq. 7) and singled out an advective term that precisely cancels that coming from
div.formula: Ȧk = ∂ t A k + j v j ∂ j A k
div.p: Hence, we are left with
div.formula: dΓ γ dt = ˛γ k ( πk + ∂ t A k )dx k + - ˛γ kj dx k v j ∂ k π j + - ˛γ kj dx k v j B kj
div.p: On the other hand, Eq. 34 and Eq. 7 give
div.formula: πk = (∂ k A 0 -∂ t A k ) + j v j B kj -∂ k (V + Q) hence dΓ γ dt = ˛γ k dx k ∂ k ( A 0 -V -Q) ≡ 0
div.p: which is again Kelvin circulation theorem: circulation is conserved along any dynamically evolved path -hence continuous in time -and no problem arises from the possible presence of singularities which, too, evolve during the dynamics. Note, however, that this results does not conflict with the formation of vortexes. Vortexes can emerge in pairs with opposite chirality, or they can manifest as closed vortex lines originating from a single point [73]. The distinction from a classical, inviscid fluid lies in the fact that in QHD extended vortex structures (such as line vortexes in three dimensions) are anticipated, whereas in a classical fluid, the concept of a line vortex does not appear to be a plausible dynamical limit [40].

In closing this Appendix we notice that the particle trajectories define useful paths to synthesize the wavefunction [30]. Indeed, according to the continuity equation in Lagrangian form, Eq. 37, we can write
div.formula: n(x t , t) = n(x 0 , t 0 ) exp - ˆt t0 (∇v) (x τ , τ ) dτ (A1)
div.p: where the divergence is evaluated along the trajectory x t started from x 0 at t = t 0 . Likewise, we have
div.formula: S(x t , t) = S(x 0 , t 0 ) + ˆt t0 L(x τ , τ ) dτ (A2)
div.p: where L is a pseudo-electromagnetic Lagrangian in which V -A 0 +Q plays the role of potential (see the Hamilton-Jacobi equation of Eq. 15). Hence,
div.formula: ψ(x t , t) = exp - 1 2 ˆt t0 (∇v) (x τ , τ ) dτ (A3) × exp i ˆt t0 L(x τ , τ ) dτ ψ(x 0 , t 0 ) (A4)
div.p: builds the wavefunction amplitude along the trajectory x t that solves the QHD equations of motion. Noteworthy, the "propagator" appearing here is determined by a single trajectory, in striking contrast with a Feymann's path integral where the propagator is the sum of contributions from infinitely many paths. This might be useful for interpretative purposes but it is of limited help in practice since, as emphasized in the main text, it is not possible to solve the QHD equations for a single quantum trajectory, unless the evolving density is known in advance.

div.head: Appendix B: Wigner function and momentum moments
div.p: Momentum moments represent an essential ingredient for the development of a hydrodynamical picture of quantum dynamics beyond the pure state case. With their help one can recast the evolution of a quantum system in terms of a hierarchical set of equations for the momentum moments, where each moment is seen to couple to the next (higher order) one. For pure states the hierarchy can be truncated at the first moment equation -second and higher moments of a pure state can be expressed in terms of the zero-th and the first moment alone -but in general the whole structure of the hierarchy is needed and often a closure is pragmatically introduced to make the problem manageable. The purpose of this Appendix is to extend such construction to the case where the quantum system is subjected to gauge fields (in addition to the usual scalar potential typically considered) which make the standard construction of limited help (since it is gauge dependent). Hence, we define here a new type of gauge invariant moments -which we call mechanical moment moments (MMMs) because of their relation with the mechanical momentum operator -and show that their equation of motion take a simple, physically sound form. We start with some general remarks about the momentum moments and recast them in a form that suit better to the presence of gauge fields. Henceforth, we shall use the hat on operators to denote the abstract operators and and not their Schrödinger representation.
We recall that for a D dimensional quantum system described by a continuous position variable x ∈ R D the Wigner function ρ W is defined as a Fourier transform of the function
div.formula: W (q, r) = q - r 2 |ρ|q + r 2 = ρ q - r2
div.p: , q + r 2 with respect to r, at fixed q. Here, ρ is the system density operator and ρ(x, x ′ ) = x|ρ|x ′ is the density matrix (hence, r ≡ x ′x the "difference variable" or "relative position"). That is, ρ W (q, p) = ˆdD r (2π ) D W (q, r)e i pr in such a way that ρ W (q, p) is a kind of distribution function in phase space, (q, p) ∈ R 2D . Then, the momentum moments are the moments of this "distribution" at fixed q
div.formula: S (n) i1i2..iD (q) = ˆdD p p i1 1 p i2 2 ..p iD D ρ W (q, p)
div.p: (irrespective of the fact that ρ W is a true distribution or not) and can be obtained from the s-derivatives at s = 0 of the characteristic function
div.formula: F q (s) = e isp/ q ≡ ˆdD pe isp/ ρ W (q, s) s ∈ R D
div.p: where .. denotes the average w.r.t. to the Wigner "distribution". Specifically,
div.formula: F q (s) = ∞ n=0 i n (sp) n n!
div.p: where
div.formula: (sp) n = [n] i1i2..iD n! i 1 !i 2 !..i D ! (s 1 p 1 ) i1 (s 2 p 2 ) i2 ..(s S p D ) iD
div.p: and [n] stands for the constraint
div.formula: D k=1 i k = n.
div.p: In other words we have
div.formula: F q (s) = ∞ i1i2..iD i n S (n) i1i2..iD (q) i 1 !i 2 !..i D ! s i1 1 s i2 2 ..s iD D with n = k i k and then i n S (n) i1i2..iD (q) = ∂ n ∂ i1 s1 ∂ i2 s2 ..∂ iD sD F q (s)| s=0 (B1)
div.p: By definition, the characteristic function is the Fourier transform (w.r.t. to p) of the function ρ W (q, p), which is itself the Fourier transform of W (q, r). Hence,
div.formula: F q (s) ≡ ρ q + s 2 , q - s 2
div.p: and the moments are seen to characterize the behavior of the density matrix along the second diagonal of the (x, x ′ ) "plane", for a fixed position q along the first (main) diagonal. The characteristic function can be conveniently given in terms of translational operators, namely
div.formula: F q (s) ≡ q|T -s/2 ρT -s/2 |q = lim x→q,x ′ →q x|T -s/2 ρT -s/2 |x ′
div.p: where T a = exp(-iap/ ) is the translation operator by a, T a |x = |x + a (or equivalently, x|T a ψ = ψ(xa)).
The usefulness of the representation in the rightmost side of the last equation becomes evident when expanding the exponentials
div.formula: x|T -s/2 ρT -s/2 |x ′ = ∞ n,m=0 1 n!m! x| isp 2 n ρ isp 2 m |x ′
div.p: and remembering that
div.formula: x|p i |ψ = (-i )∂ i ψ ψ|p i |x ′ = (i )∂ ′ i ψ ′ *
div.p: where we have introduced the notation ψ ≡ ψ(x) and ψ ′ ≡ ψ(x ′ ) for the wavefunction and, correspondingly,
div.formula: ∂ i ≡ ∂/∂x i and ∂ ′ i ≡ ∂/∂x ′ i . It is then clear that P (0) (q) = q|ρ|q ≡ n(q) P(1)
div.p: where [.., ..] + is the anti-commutator. Here, we have introduced new moments P (n) ijk.. in which the subscripts denote explicitly the order of the products, i.e. the momentum operators appearing in the nested products are labeled with an index from right to left, starting from the innermost product. This is useless in this case since the pk 's commute with each other -meaning that the newly defined moments are fully symmetric w.r.t. their indexes -but it will become important soon when we generalize this construction to non-commuting operator. This representation of moments in terms of symmetrized product turns out to be useful in view of the following property
div.formula: q|[p k , Ô] + |q = (p k + p ′ * k )O(x, x ′ )| x=x ′ =q
div.p: which holds for arbitrary operators Ô. Here
div.formula: p k = -i ∂ k , p ′ k = -i ∂ ′
div.p: k are the Schrödinger-representation momentum operators and O(x, x ′ ) = x| Ô|x ′ . Indeed, it allows us to re-write the above moments in the form
div.formula: P (n) ijk.. (q) = p i + p ′ * i 2 p j + p ′ * j 2 p k + p ′ * k 2 ... .. ρ(x, x ′ )| x=x ′ =q (B3)
div.p: which makes evident the symmetry of the moments (the p k 's commute with each other and with the p ′ j 's). Furthermore, the last equation provides a useful expression for the moments
or, equivalently,
div.formula: P (n) ijk.. (q) = (-i ) n ∂ si ∂ sj ∂ s k ..ρ q + s 2 , q - s 2
div.p: which is the same as Eq. B1. As mentioned above, these moments are dynamically coupled to each other. The (infinite) set of equation of motion can be obtained in rather general form if the system Hamiltonian takes the standard form
div.formula: H = p2 2m + V
div.p: where V is a scalar potential, see Refs. [44,74,75]. We now consider the case of a quantum mechanical system subjected to gauge fields, in addition to the scalar potential V, that is, we take our usual Hamiltonian
div.formula: H = ij ξ ij 2 πi πj + (V -A 0 )
div.p: with [76] πk = pk -A k . Furthermore, to distinguish this case from the previous one we shall use σ in place of ρ for the density operator and, correspondingly, σ(x, x ′ ) in place of ρ(x, x ′ ) for the density matrices. Clearly, under such circumstances, the above momentum moments P (n) ijk.. (q) are of limited help since they are gauge dependent and cannot provide a gauge invariant formulation of the dynamics. Progress can be made by generalizing the anticommutator-based definition, Eq. B2, namely
Equivalently, upon noticing
div.formula: x|[π k , Ô] + |x ′ = x|π k Ô|x ′ + x ′ |π k Ô † |x * = (π k + π ′ * k )O(x, x ′ )
div.p: we can write (cf. Eq. B3)
div.formula: M (n) ijk.. (q) = π i + π ′ * i 2 π j + π ′ * j 2 π k + π ′ * k 2 ... ... σ(x, x ′ )| x=x ′ =q
div.p: where now
div.formula: π k = -i ∂ k -A k , π ′ k = -i ∂ ′ k -A ′ k and A k ≡ A k (x), A ′ k ≡ A k (x ′
div.p: ). Since the π k 's are gauge covariant, these moments are, by construction, gauge invariant and they thus fulfill our requirement. The price to be paid is that they are no longer symmetric in their indexes since
div.formula: [π j +π ′ * j , π k +π ′ * k ] = [π j , π k ]+[π ′ * j , π ′ * k ] ≡ -i (B jk -B ′ jk )
div.p: where again we have primed (unprimed) quantities for functions of x ′ (x). For instance, a direct calculation shows that
div.formula: M (3) ijk -M (3) ikj = - i 2 2 ∂ i B jk (q) M (0) (q) M (4) mijk -M (4) mikj = - i 2 2 ∂ i B jk (q) M (1) m (q) + +∂ m B jk (q) M (1) i
div.p: The exception is the symmetry in the two leftmost indexes
div.formula: M (n) ijk.. (q) = π j + π ′ * j 2 π i + π ′ * i 2 π k + π ′ * k 2 .. .. σ(x, x ′ )| x=x ′ =q + -i B ij -B ′ ij π k + π ′ * k 2 .. σ(x, x ′ )| x=x ′ =q ≡ M (n)
div.p: jik.. (q) which holds for any moment since the second term disappears in the limit x = x ′ = q. We note that we could define moments which are yet gauge invariant and symmetric in their indexes, for instance
div.formula: N (n) ijk.. (q) = p i + p ′ * i 2 -A i (q) p j + p ′ * j 2 -A j (q) p k + p ′ * k 2 -A k (q) .. σ(x, x ′ )| x=x ′ =q
div.p: However, as it will become soon evident, only for the M (n) ijk.. 's defined above the equations of motion take a simple form.
To obtain the dynamical equations for the moments we need the Liouville -von Neumann (LvN) equation [77]
div.formula: i ∂ t σ = [H, σ] ≡ ij ξ ij 2 [π i [π j , σ] + ] + [V -A 0 , σ] the identities x|[ φ, Ô] ± |x ′ = (φ ± φ ′ ) O(x, x ′ ) x|[π k , Ô] ± |x ′ = π k ± π ′ * k O(x, x ′ )
div.p: (which hold for any operator Ô and an arbitrary local operator φ), the commutation properties
div.formula: [π j + π ′ * j , π k ± π ′ * k ] = i (B jk ∓ B ′ * jk ) [π j + π ′ * j , φ ± φ ′ * ] = -i (∂ j φ ∓ ∂ j φ ′ * )
div.p: and
div.formula: (∂ k + ∂ ′ k )O(x, x ′ )| x=x ′ =q = ∂ q k O(q, q)
div.p: In the above, the kinetic energy contribution of the LvN has been re-written in the convenient form
div.formula: [T, σ] ≡ 1 2 ij ξ ij [π i [π j , σ] + ]
div.p: that can be easily checked with a direct calculation. The equation of motion for the 0 th moment can be obtained readily upon taking the q| ..|q matrix element of LvN and noticing that
div.formula: q|[V -A 0 , σ]|q = 0 and that q|[π i , [π j , σ] + ]|q = π i -π ′ * i π j + π ′ * j σ(x, x ′ )| x=x ′ =q = -i (∂ i + ∂ ′ i ) π j + π ′ * j σ(x, x ′ )| x=x ′ =q ≡ -2i ∂ qi M (1) j (q)
div.p: It takes the form [78]
div.formula: ∂M (0) ∂t (q) = - ij ξ ij ∂ i M(1) j (q)
div.p: The equation of motion for the first order moments follows similarly. For, on the one hand, we have
and, on the other hand,
div.formula: q|[π k , ∂ t σ] + |q = - i 2 ij ξ ij 2 q|[π k , [π i , [π j , σ] + ]] + |q = - i 2 q|[π k , [V -A 0 , σ]] + |q
div.p: Here the first term reads as
div.formula: - i 2 ij ξ ij 2 (π k + π ′ * k )(π i -π ′ * i )(π j + π ′ * j )σ(x, x ′ )| x=x ′ =q = = - i 2 ij ξ ij 2 (π i -π ′ * i )(π k + π ′ * k )(π j + π ′ * j )σ(x, x ′ )| x=x ′ =q + - i ij ξ ij 2 i (B ki + B ′ ki ) 2 (π j + π ′ * j )σ(x, x ′ )| x=x ′ =q = ≡ - ij ξ ij ∂ qi M (2) kj (q) + ij ξ ij B ki (q)M(1)
j (q)
div.p: where we have used
div.formula: [(π k +π ′ * k ), (π i -π ′ * i )] = i (B ki +B ′ ki )
div.p: , while for the second term we find
Hence, summing up we arrive at
div.formula: ∂M (1) k ∂t (q) = F k - ij ξ ij ∂ i M(2) kj (q)
div.p: where
div.formula: F k (q) = (-∂ k V(q)+E k )M (0) (q)+ ij ξ ij B ki (q)M (1) j (q)
div.p: is the density of classical forces,
div.formula: E k = (∂ k A 0 -∂ t A k )
div.p: being the pseudo-electric field.
The equation for the third moment can be derived straightforwardly along steps similar to those followed above. It is only considerably longer and thus here we only quote the final result
div.formula: ∂M (2) km ∂t (q) = - ij ξ ij ∂ i M(3)
kmj (q)+ + ij ξ ij B ki (q)M (2) mj (q) + B mi (q)M(2)
kj (q) + (-∂ k V + E k ) M (1) m (q) + (-∂ m V + E m ) M(1)
div.p: k (q) which shows the "typical" classical contribution, comprising the intrinsic force and the gauge forces, and the coupling to the higher order moment. Please notice that this equation is symmetric w.r.t. the indexes k and m, as it should be since the moment is yet of second order. However, the third moment M
(3) kmj appearing in the first line of the r.h.s. of this equation cannot be replaced by its symmetric counterpart N
(3) kmj , hence the latter cannot be used to build up a hierarchy of equations of motion.
To summarize, the equations of motion for the first few moments are
div.formula: ∂M (0) ∂t (q) = - ij ξ ij ∂ i M(1)
j (q) ∂M (1) k ∂t (q) = - ij ξ ij ∂ i M(2)
kj (q)+ + (-∂ k V(q) + E k )M (0) (q)+ + ij ξ ij B ki (q)M(1)
j (q) ∂M (2) km ∂t (q) = - ij ξ ij ∂ i M(3)
kmj (q)+ + (-∂ k V + E k ) M (1) m (q)+ + (-∂ m V + E m ) M (1) k (q)+ + ij ξ ij B ki (q)M (2) mj (q) + B mi (q)M (2) kj (q)
div.p: ... In general, for the n th moment we have an "upward" coupling to the (n + 1) th layer of moments that occurs through a surface term containing the spatial derivatives of the (n+1) th moments, and both "downward" and "horizontal" couplings that occur through "classical" forces. The rank-1 pseudo-electric field E k and the intrinsic force -∂ k V link the n th layer of moments to the layer below it. The rank-2 pseudo-magnetic field B kj increases the order by one and thus describes "horizontal" coupling to moments of the same order n (starting from n = 1).

In the above hydrodynamic set of equations the first is continuity equation, with J i (q) = j ξ ij M (1) j (q) playing the role of density current. The second equation extends the quantum Navier-Stokes equation given in the main text for a pure state, namely
div.formula: ∂ t (nπ k ) = F k - j ∂ j (nv j π k ) + j ∂ j σ j k
div.p: where F k = nF k is the density of classical forces. Indeed, with the notation of the main text,
div.formula: - j ∂ j (nv j π k ) + j ∂ j σ j k ≡ - ij ξ ij ∂ j nπ i π k + nw i w k - 2 4 ∂ i ∂ k n
div.p: and the term between brackets on the r.h.s. is the moment M
ki (q) for a pure state. This can be checked with a simple calculation: setting σ = |ψ ψ|
div.formula: M (2) kj (q) = π k + π ′ * k 2 π j + π ′ * j 2 ψ(x)ψ * (x)| x=x ′ =q
div.p: we recognize the complex-valued momentum fields of the main text (evaluated at both x and x ′ )
div.formula: π j + π ′ * j 2 ψ(x)ψ * (x) = Π j + Π ′ * j 2 ψ(x)ψ * (x)
div.p: Thus, with the help of the commutation properties
div.formula: [π k + π ′ * k , Π j + Π ′ * j ] = -i (∂ k Π j -∂ ′ k Π ′ * j ) (which reduce to 2 ∂ k ℑΠ j = 2 ∂ k w j ≡ -2 ∂ k ∂ j ln n in the required limit x = x ′ = q) we find M (2) kj (q) = Π j + Π ′ * j 2 Π k + Π ′ * k 2 ψ(x)ψ * (x)| x=x ′ =q + - 2 4 n ∂ k ∂ j ln n ≡ π j π k n - 2 4 n ∂ k ∂ j ln n = π j π k n - 2 4 ∂ k ∂ j n - ∂ k n∂ j n n
div.p: where now π k = ℜΠ k in the notation of the main text, and we remember -/2∂ k ln n ≡ w k . This analysis is insightful, since in the last expression the first term is a "uncorrelated" term, while the term between brackets is right the one responsible for the surface forces. Specifically, upon setting (in current notation), C
div.formula: kj (q) = M (2) kj (q) - M(2)
div.p: we have for a pure state
div.formula: - ij ξ ij ∂ i C kj = F S k ≡ i ∂ i σ i k
div.p: where σ i k is the quantum stress tensor. This suggests to rewrite the general first moment equation (i.e. that for arbitrary mixed states) introducing the rank-2 tensor C
(2) kl (q) defined as in the above equation. The result is
div.formula: dπ k dt (q) = - 1 M (0) (q) ij ξ ij ∂ i C (2) kj (q)+ + (-∂ k V(q) + E k ) + j B kj (q)v j (q)
div.p: where π k = M

k /M (0) and v j = i ξ ji π i are the usual momentum and velocity fields, and d/dt is the material derivative. Written in this way the first moment equation describes the Lagrangian-frame evolution of momentum in terms of classical "bulk" forces and a surface force. Whether the system is in a pure or in a mixed state it is the latter responsible for quantum effects.

More generally, we argue that a similar decomposition is possible for higher order moments. That is, we expect them to contain all quantum effects in a surface term that can be singled out by subtracting the uncorrelated contributions from their definition. One can then envisage different "classical closures" to the hydrodynamic hierarchy, depending on the momentum order n at which the surface term is forced to be null. Classical mechanics would result upon setting C (2) = 0 whereas C (n) = 0 for n > 2 would describe a classical world with quantum corrections of the (n -2) th order.

where the effective Liouville super-operator is the sum of three gauge invariant contributions. Here, the third term can be shown to be the derivative of contributions that appear in the second term, namely
div.formula: - 2 2 ij ξ ij ∂ i (|D j u(x) u(x)| -|u(x) D j u(x)|)
div.p: and these (gauge invariant) contributions (i.e. the terms between brackets in the last equation) turn out to be
div.formula: |D j u(x) u(x)| -|u(x) D j u(x)| ≡ [∂ j ρ el , ρ el ]
div.p: To prove these identities we first notice that
div.formula: Q |D i D j u u| -|u D i D j u| Q = ( Q |∂ i D j u u| -|u ∂ i D j u| Q ) + iA i (|D j u u| + |u D j u|)
div.p: where Q |D j u = |D j u . Then, we move the derivatives in the first term out of the kets and the bras
div.formula: Q |∂ i D j u u| -|u ∂ i D j u| Q = ∂ i (|D j u u| -|u D j u|)+ -(∂ i Q) |D j u u| + |u D j u| (∂ i Q)+ -|D j u ∂ i u| + |∂ i u D j u| and notice that -∂ i Q = ∂ i P = |∂ i u u| + |u ∂ i u| to write -(∂ j Q) |D j u u| + |u D j u| (∂ j Q) = ρ el ( ∂ i u|D j u -D j u|∂ i u ) = ρ el ( D i u|D j u -D j u|D i u )
div.p: since u|D j u ≡ 0. On the other hand, we also have
div.formula: -|D j u ∂ i u| + |∂ i u D j u| = -|D j u D i u| + |D i u D j u|+ -iA i (|D j u u| + |u D j u|)
div.p: hence, summing up, we obtain
div.formula: Q |D i D j u u| -|u D i D j u| Q = ∂ i (|D j u u| -|u D j u|)+ -|D j u D i u| + |D i u D j u|
div.p: where the last two terms cancel each other once multiplied by the (symmetric) inverse mass tensor ξ ij and summed over i and j. This proves the first identity. As for the second identity a direct calculation gives
div.formula: [∂ j ρ el , ρ el ] = ∂ j ρ el |u u| -|u u| ∂ j ρ el = = |∂ j u u| -|u ∂ j u| + + |u ∂ j u|u u| -|u u|∂ j u u| ≡ |D j u u| -|u D j u|
div.p: since ∂ j u|u = u|∂ j u * = iA j and u|∂ j u = -iA j . Noteworthy, since in fact we have ∂ j ρ el ≡ |D j u u| + |u D j u| we find that the gauge invariant dyad |D j u u| can be written solely in terms of ρ el and its derivatives
div.formula: |D j u u| = 1 2 (∂ j ρ el + [∂ j ρ el , ρ el ])
div.p: and similarly for its adjoint.
As a second step we prove that the equation of motion
div.formula: i ρel = [H el , ρ el ] + j u j [∂ j ρ el , ρ el ]+ - 2 2 ij ξ ij ∂ i ([∂ j ρ el , ρ el ])
div.p: is trace and purity conserving since, as mentioned in the main text, this suffices to show its equivalence to the original equation, Eq. 92, for the state vector. The trace conserving property is immediate since on the r.h.s. there appear only commutators, and these are traceless by virtue of the trace properties. As for purity, we need to prove that Tr (ρ el ρel ) = 0
Left multiplication by ρ el gives three terms, the first two of which are easily seen to vanish
div.formula: Tr (ρ el [A, ρ el ]) = Tr (ρ el Aρ el ) -Tr (ρ el ρ el A) = Tr (ρ el ρ el A) -Tr (ρ el ρ el A)
div.p: by cycling under the trace operation. As for the third contribution, we notice that
div.formula: ρ el ∂ i ([∂ j ρ el , ρ el ]) = ∂ i (ρ el [∂ j ρ el , ρ el ]) -(∂ i ρ el ) [∂ j ρ el , ρ el ]
div.p: and then upon tracing
div.formula: Tr (ρ el ∂ i ([∂ j ρ el , ρ el ])) = -Tr ((∂ i ρ el ) [∂ j ρ el , ρ el ])
div.p: since the first contribution vanishes as above. The remaining term gives the contributions
div.formula: 2 2 ij ξ ij Tr ((∂ i ρ el ) (∂ j ρ el ) ρ el ) + - 2 2 ij ξ ij Tr ((∂ j ρ el ) (∂ i ρ el ) ρ el ) ≡ 0
div.p: as is evident upon expanding the commutator, cycling under the trace and remembering that ξ ij = ξ ji . This shows that if ρ el represents a pure state at initial time, it will remain a pure state at any later time when evolving according to Eq. 66.
Finally, in closing this Appendix we show that the second and the third term on the r.h.s. of the equation of motion can be compound into a single term
div.formula: j u j [∂ j ρ el , ρ el ] - 2 2 ij ξ ij ∂ i ([∂ j ρ el , ρ el ]) = 2 2n ij ξ ij ∂ j (n[ρ el , ∂ j ρ el ])
div.p: and, interestingly, the latter can be recast as an electronnuclear correction to the Hamiltonian that defines the Liouvillian, namely
div.formula: i ρel = [H el + δH en , ρ el ]
div.p: where
div.formula: δH en = - 2 2n ij ξ ij ∂ i (n∂ j ρ el )
div.p: The first step is simple, and requires just to recognize
div.formula: u j = i ξ ij w i = -2 i ξ ij ∂ i ln n.
div.p: As for the second result, we notice that
div.formula: - 2 2n ij ξ ij [∂ i (n∂ j ρ el ), ρ el ] = = - 2 2n ij ξ ij [∂ i (n∂ j ρ el ρ el ) -∂ i (ρ el n∂ j ρ el )+ -n(∂ j ρ el )(∂ i ρ el ) + n(∂ i ρ el )(∂ j ρ el )] ≡ 2 2n ij ξ ij ∂ i (n[ρ el , ∂ j ρ el ])
div.p: since the terms in the last line of the second step give no contributions to the sum.

div.head: Appendix D: Electronic moments
div.p: In the exact factorization of the electron-nuclear wavefunction describing a pure state, the doubly parametrized electronic dyad
plays a key role, in that it determines through its diagonal elements the conditional electronic density operator of the system and, in general, through its trace, the true nuclear density matrix
div.formula: ρ n (x, x ′ ) = ψ(x)ψ * (x ′ ) u(x ′ )|u(x) = σ(x, x ′ )Tr e Ŵ(x, x ′ )
div.p: where σ(x, x ′ ) = ψ(x)ψ * (x ′ ) is the apparent nuclear density matrix. The purpose of this Appendix is to generalize this concept to the case where a single nuclear wavefunction can be yet singled out from an ensemble, whereas the electronic state is in an intrinsically mixed state. That is, the total density operator of the system is taken of the form (in the Schrödinger representation for the nuclei)

where Ŵ is the ensemble average of the operator defined above. In other words, given a total density operator ρ(x, x ′ ) and a nuclear wavefunction ψ(x) (hence, the "pure-state" σ(x, x ′ ) = ψ(x)ψ * (x ′ )) we introduce a "conditional electronic operator" according to
div.formula: Ŵ(x, x ′ ) = ρ(x, x ′ ) σ(x, x ′ )
div.p: for any x and x ′ where the wavefunction does not vanish. Note that our assumption about the existence of a single nuclear wavefunction implies that the electronic states involved in the ensemble (call them |u α (x) , with α = 1, 2, ..) have only an overall gauge degree of freedom, i.e. their phases cannot be changed independently of each other. Rather, a single set of gauge fields exist, A k , irrespective of α.
Next, we define moments of these conditional electronic operators (which we call electronic moments) by analogy with the momentum moments introduced in Appendix C, that is,
div.formula: M(0) (q) = Ŵ(q, q) M(1) k (q) = µ k + µ ′ * k 2 Ŵ(x, x ′ )| x=x ′ =q M(2) kj (q) = µ k + µ ′ * k 2 µ j + µ ′ * j 2 Ŵ(x, x ′ )| x=x ′ =q ... where µ k = -i ∂ k + A k ≡ -i D k and D k = ∂ k + iA k
div.p: is the gauge covariant derivative, using the gauge potential common to the ensemble elements. By definition, these moments are gauge invariant with respect to the gauge transformations mentioned above (notice the sign change of the gauge field when compared to the mechanical momenta π k 's, i.e., µ k ≡ -π * k ). For a pure state, for instance, we find
div.formula: M(0) (q) = |u(q) u(q)| = ρ el (q) M(1) k (q) = - i 2 (|D k u(q) u(q)| -|u(q) D k u(q)|) M(2) kj (q) = 2 4 (|D k u(q) D j u(q)| + |D j u(q) D k u(q)|) + - 2 4 (|D k D j u(q) u(q)| + |u(q) D k D j u(q)|)
div.p: in the same notation used in the main text. Then, we proceed in deriving the equations of motion for these moments. We start from the Lagrangian-frame pure-state result
div.formula: i ρel = [H el , ρ el ]+ + j u j (|D j u(x) u(x)| -|u(x) D j u(x)|) + - 2 2 ij ξ ij ∂ i (|D j u u| -|u D j u|)
div.p: and re-interpret it in terms of moments,
div.formula: d M(0) dt (q) = - i [H el (q), M(0) (q)]+ + 2   j u j M(1) j (q) -2 ij ξ ij ∂ i M(1) j (q)   Here, u j = i ξ ij w i = -2 i ξ ij ∂ i ln n, thus i d M(0) dt (q) = [H el (q), M(0) (q)] - i n ij ξ ij ∂ i n M(1)
div.p: j This equation survives to the kind of ensemble average we are interested in (being linear in the M's ), hence it represents the equation of motion of our zero-th order electronic moment (the conditional density operator for electrons generalized to arbitrary mixed states). The key difference with a pure state is that only for the latter the first order moment appearing on the right hand side can be expressed in terms of the zero-th order one and its spatial derivative, namely
div.formula: M(1) j = i 2 [ M(0) , ∂ j M(0) ] (pure state)
div.p: Henceforth, we shall proceed by looking at the pure state case and seeking a form of the equations of motion that suits ("survives") to the kind of ensemble average mentioned above.
The dynamical equation of the first order moments follows from the observation that
div.formula: ∂ M(1) k ∂t (q) = ∂ t + ∂ ′ t µ k + µ ′ * k 2 |u u ′ | | x=x ′ =q,t=t ′ ≡ ∂ t A k |u u| + + µ k + µ ′ * k 2 ∂ t + ∂ ′ t |u u ′ | | x=x ′ =q,t=t ′ since [∂ t + ∂ ′ t , µ k + µ ′ * k ] = ∂ t A k + ∂ ′ t A ′ k
div.p: , where now primed and unprimed quantities refer to (x ′ , t ′ ) and (x, t), respectively. On the other hand,
div.formula: ∂ t + ∂ ′ t |u u ′ | = |D 0 u u ′ | + |u D ′ 0 u ′ | + -iA 0 |u u ′ | + iA ′ 0 |u u ′ | where D 0 = ∂ t + iA 0 is the gauge covariant time- derivative. Now, upon using [µ k + µ ′ * k , A 0 -A ′ 0 ] = -i (∂ k A 0 + ∂ ′ k A ′ 0 ), in the limit x = x ′ = q and t = t ′ we obtain ∂ M(1) k ∂t (q) = -E k M(0) (q)+ + π k + π ′ * k 2 (|D 0 u u ′ | + |u D ′ 0 u ′ |) | x=x ′ =q,t=t ′ (D1)
div.p: where E k is the pseudo-electric field. We then use the electronic equation of the exact factorization in gauge covariant form (as explained in the main text, see Eq. 82)
div.formula: i |D 0 u = (H el -V) |u -i j V j |D j u - 2 2 ij ξ ij |D i D j u
div.p: and consider first the simplified situation where we neglect the term which is of second order in the spatial derivatives of the electronic state (third term on the r.h.s.). With the replacement V j = v j + iu j and |D 0 u ≈ -i (H el -V) |uj v j |D j ui j u j |D j u the r.h.s. of Eq. D1 is seen to contain three terms. The first is the Halmitonian driving
div.formula: I = 1 2 (|D k u u ′ | (H ′ el -V ′ ) -|u D ′ k (H ′ el -V ′ )u ′ | + -|D k (H el -V)u u ′ | + (H el -V) |u D ′ k u ′ |)
div.p: where
div.formula: |D k (H el -V)u = (H el -V) |D k u + ∂ k (H el -V) |u hence I = 1 2 ([|D k u u ′ | -|u D ′ k u ′ |](H ′ el -V ′ )+ -(H el -V)[|D k u u ′ | -|u D ′ k u ′ |] -∂ k (H el -V) |u u ′ | -|u u ′ | ∂ ′ k (H ′ el -V ′ ))

div.formula: I = - i [H el , M(1)

Summing up the contributions we find

If we considered the neglected term (second order in the spatial derivatives) we would have the additional contribution i 2
div.formula: µ k + µ ′ * k 2 ij ξ ij (|D i D j u u ′ | + -|u D ′ i D ′ j u ′ | | x=x ′ =q = 2 4 ij ξ ij L kij
div.p: where now
div.formula: L kij = (D k -D ′ * k )(|D i D j u u| -|u D ′ i D ′ j u ′ |)
div.p: The latter can be re-arranged as follows
div.formula: L kij = (D k -D ′ * k ) 2 [(D i -D ′ * i )(D j + D ′ * j )+ + (D i + D ′ * i )(D j -D ′ * j )] |u u ′ |
div.p: with the usual limit implicit, and the sum of derivatives can be moved to the leftmost position -upon introducing the appropriate commutators -while the moments can be easily identified, since they only involve differences of derivatives. The result is
div.formula: L kij = 1

div.p: This concludes our derivation of the first two moment equations. We stress once again that these equations of motion are most naturally formulated in the Lagrangian frame, which is in motion on the nuclear trajectories.
Appendix E: Lie-Trotter identity
The Lie-Trotter identity is d dλ e A(λ) = ˆ1 0 dt e tA(λ) dA dλ e (1-t)A(λ) (E1)

and can be proved using the operator identity d dt e tA(λ ′ ) e (1-t)A(λ) = e tA(λ ′ ) (A(λ ′ ) -A(λ)) e (1-t)A(λ) upon integrating over t ∈ [0, 1] to write e A(λ ′ )e A(λ) = ˆ1 0 dt e tA(λ ′ ) (A(λ ′ ) -A(λ)) e (1-t)A(λ)
Indeed, setting λ ′ = λ + ∆λ and taking the limit of the incremental ratio where δf j = f j -F 0 j and F 0 j = β -1 ∂ j ln Z is the ensemble-average of the microscopic force f j .

Here, G + (E) is the retarded Green operator of the (many-body) electronic Hamiltonian, G + (E) = lim ǫ→0 + (E + iǫ -H el ) -1 = 1 i lim ǫ→0 + ˆ∞ 0 e -ǫt e -i (H el -E)t dt and the second equality follows upon splitting it into real (or principal) part and imaginary one, namely with the help of
div.formula: G + (E) = G P (E) -iπδ(E -H el )
div.p: The kernels thus introduced are the ensemble averaged generalization of the kernel introduced in Ref. [28] (Section IV.C) and represent a frequency-dependent quantum geometric tensor
div.formula: q β kj (ω) = n p n u n |(∂ k H el )Q n G + (E n + ω)|∂ j u n
div.p: and the true, frequency-dependent friction tensor
div.formula: γ β kj (ω) = -π n p n u n |(∂ k H el )Q n δ( ω -H n el )|∂ j u n = π ω n p n u n |(∂ k H el )Q n δ( ω -H n el )(∂ j H el )|u n = π ω m =n m,n p n (f k ) nm (f j ) mn δ(E n + ω -E m )
div.p: where for the second equality we have used δ(E n + ω -H el )Q n |∂ j u n = -1 ω δ(E n + ω -H el )Q n (∂ j H el ) |u n , and for the third one we have expanded Q n in the energy basis and introduced the matrix elements of the force (f k ) nm = u n |(-∂ k H el )|u m . In the Markov limit we have
div.formula: q β kj (ω) → q β kj = n p n ∂ k u n |Q n |∂ j u n = n p n D n k u n |D n j u n
div.p: which amounts to the averaged quantum geometric tensor of Eq. 84,
div.formula: q av kj = n p n D k u n |D j u n
div.p: to within a minor correction in its symmetric part, q β kj = q av kj + n p n δA n k δA n j where δA n k = A n k -A k . Hence, ℑq β kj = ℑq av kj and the pseudo-magnetic field contribution is precisely that described by the ensemble averaged geometric tensor.

As for the Markovian friction, the physical friction kernel η kj is given by the real part of γ kj = lim ω→0 2γ β kj (ω) + 2γ β kj (-ω) 2 as extensively discussed in Ref. [28] (see Appendix E). In the previous work we found γ kj = lim ω→0 γ kj (|ω|) since γ kj (-|ω|) ≡ 0 at T = 0 K where only the ground-state is populated. Now this has to be replaced by for any temperature but the smallest one, and the zerofrequency limit needs to be carefully addressed. This result follows from the detailed-balance condition on the kernel, a kind of Kubo-Martin-Schwinger relationship γ β kj (ω) = -e β ω γ β jk (-ω) that can be proved with a direct calculation in the energy basis Notice that γ kj is symmetric in its real part and antisymmetric in its imaginary one. Clearly, the difference with the T = 0 K case is due to the electronic deexcitation processes that can occur in the electronic bath at finite temperature and that work by pumping energy into the nuclear system. Detailed balance guarantees that the kernel ℜγ kj (ω) remains positive definite for ω > 0, hence it correctly describes friction.
div.formula: η kj = lim

div.head: Appendix G: Independent electrons
div.p: In this Appendix we focus on independent electrons, and address the issue of obtaining the finite-temperature Markovian friction kernel η kj for them. We start from the results of Section F in the form η kj = lim ω→0 ℜη kj (ω) where η kj (ω) = γ β kj (ω)(1e -β ω ) and
div.formula: γ β kj (ω) = π ω n p n u n |(∂ k H el )Q n δ( ω -H n el )(∂ j H el )|u n
div.p: Here, n labels energy eigenstates, p n are Boltzmann populations, Q n = 1 -|u n u n | and H n el = E n -H el . For N independent electrons the many-body states are Hartree-Fock "determinants" built with the single-particle states |φ a of the monoelectronic Hamiltonian h, and the sum over states can be recast as where I is a collection of N -1 particle labels, ǫ a are single-particle energies and ∆ǫ ba = ǫ bǫ a . In obtaining the above expression we have exploited Slater rules and included only single-particle excitations, i.e., Ia → Ib. This automatically accounts for the projector Q n without further constraining the single-particle labels [a = b in unnecessary in the above expression unless ω ≡ 0]. In the thermodynamic limit we have The latter kernel satisfies η kj (ω) * = η kj (-ω) = η jk (ω), which makes its real part symmetric in its indexes, and even in ω. The Markovian friction kernel follows unambiguously as a zero frequency limit, The latter form is particularly useful for taking the T = 0 K limit, where
div.formula: η kj = lim
- f (ǫ a + ω) -f (ǫ a ) ω → δ(ǫ a -µ)
div.p: and µ → ǫ F , the Fermi energy. Hence,
div.formula: η kj = π a,b D k ab D j ba δ(ǫ a -ǫ F )δ(ǫ b -ǫ F )
div.p: since the sum becomes real in this limit. This is the Head-Gordon Tully result [72], who were the first to derive an electronic friction kernel by working at zero temperature in the independent electron approximation.
where only the first two terms are gauge invariant. The last term must be combined with the Berry connection to form the electron-dynamical force F ED k .
[49] To obtain this equation we exploited the geometrical identity ℜ ∂ k u|QDiDj u = 1 2 (∂jg ik + ∂ig kj -∂ k gij ),see Ref. [28].
[50] In our previous work, we called δF BO k a "genuine non-Born-Oppenheimer force" and used the symbol F NBO k for it.

[51] In other words, this magnetic force only appears in the Euler frame where x is fixed and the fluid elements move with speed v i (x, t). In the Lagrangian frame xt is at rest with the moving fluid, and cannot couple to the magnetic field.
[52] These properties guarantee that ρ el represents a purestate density at any time, provided it does at initial time.

As a corollary, the instantaneous electronic ket |ut(x) can be retrieved from ρ el (x) by projection, using an (almost) arbitrary |ψ ∈ H el . In this picture, it is the (quasi) arbitraness of |ψ that provides the gauge freedom.
[53] Please notice that the hat is used here to identify operators in the electronic variables, the density is a matrix in nuclear coordinates and an operator in the electronic ones.
[54] What is specific of the Berry connection is that it guarantees the orthogonality condition mentioned above.

div.p: Appendix C: Gauge invariant formulation of the pure-state electron dynamics

We show in this Appendix that the electronic equation of motion of the exact factorization approach, Eq. 91 for the Eulerian frame or Eq. 92 for the Lagrangian frame, is fully equivalent to Eq. 66 for the conditional electronic density operator ρ el = |u u|. We start writing

In this Appendix we provide an expression, which is valid in thermal equilibrium, for the pseudo-magnetic contribution contained in the DMS friction kernel of Eq. 114. We start from
and write the spatial derivative of the density operator at equilibrium as

We first address the I kj contribution, which explicitly reads as a sort of cross-correlation matrix
as can be seen upon using ∂ j p n = βp n u n |δf j (t)|u n . This term is vanishingly small if the microscopic forces in the energy eigenstates are close to each other,

In any case, it is symmetric in its indexes, hence it does not affect our conclusions on the pseudo-magnetic term.
